## A Day in a Life of Huge Treatment Effects

Your morning starts with wild swings in trust for your spouse: up 11% from holding a warm cup of coffee, [@williams2008] then down 30% from the smell of last night's kitchen trash. [@lee2012] Uncertain about your marriage, you pick out a red shirt for the 10% bump on the attractiveness scale [@elliot2008] and head to work. There a power pose jacks your testosterone 30%, and putting your feet up on the desk makes you 43% more likely to take a risky bet. [@carney2010] Your inevitable meeting with HR is all but guaranteed by a dim room light that made you cheat on your reimbursement forms 47% above baseline. [@zhong2010lamps] To save your job and protect your health, you duck out of work early and head to the bar. The bottle of red wine reduces your cardiovascular mortality 30% and your all-cause mortality 20%. [@ronksley2011] Somehow you make it home and vow to do better the next day. You swallow a fistful of vitamin C, [@pauling1970] echinacea, [@shah2007] zinc, [@hemila2017] and melatonin, [@herxheimer2002] all guaranteed to halve various medical and sleep problems. Exhausted, you doze off to a podcast about there being yet another study proving the existence of ESP. [@bem2011]

## Huge Silly Treatment Effects are Hallucinations from a Broken Scientific Publishing Industry {#the-publication-boundary}

Why does science keep popularizing obviously absurd claims about trivial things having large impacts? At least two reasons.

The first you've probably heard of: the *file drawer*. Researchers run many studies and only publish the ones that cross an arbitrary threshold of statistical significance. That's the diagonal line in @fig-publication-boundary. Studies below it mostly never appear; studies above it might. The bigger the sample size *N* (along the horizontal axis), the easier that threshold is to clear.

The second you might not have noticed. Small-*N* studies *cannot produce small-effect publications*. The arithmetic won't allow it. A study with *N* = 40 has to report *d* ≥ 0.62 (Cohen's *d*---treatment effect over outcome SD) to reach *p* \< 0.05, regardless of what's actually true about the world. So when small-*N* studies do publish, they publish huge effects---not because huge effects are there, but because nothing smaller can get through. All six intro findings plotted here sit in the top-left quadrant: unlikely effect sizes propped up by weak evidence.

![The p < .05 publication boundary. For a two-sample test, a study only clears the conventional threshold when observed D is roughly 2 * 1.96 / sqrt(N). The labeled points are the opening-scenario examples that map cleanly onto a single-study two-sample-style D-vs-N point.](_generated/figures/figure1_publication_boundary.png){#fig-publication-boundary}

## It\'s Not Just the Silly Treatments --- Most Huge Effects are Hallucinations {#small-n-hallucinations-are-unhelpful-and-irredeemable}

Below are 1,451 recovered original/replication endpoint pairs, now using the expanded local replication-pair build rather than the smaller draft table alone.

The pairs come from the major coordinated replication efforts: the Reproducibility Project: Psychology, Many Labs 1--5, the Social Sciences and Experimental Economics Replication Projects, the Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology, DARPA SCORE, the Cova experimental philosophy replication, the Pipeline Project, and the Registered Replication Reports.

@fig-replication-combined summarizes the current replication build. 81% of effects shrink, 42% shrink below the p \< 0.05 boundary at the replication sample size, and the fitted log-log slope implies about 53% lower D for each 10x increase in N.

![Huge effect sizes evaporate in larger replication attempts. The current recovered pair table contains 1,451 original/replication endpoint pairs; 81% shrink, 42% shrink below the p < .05 boundary, and the log-log fit implies about 53% lower D for each 10x increase in N. The marginal distributions replace the draft's old separate before/after distribution figure.](_generated/figures/figure2_replication_combined.png){#fig-replication-combined}

::: {.callout .blue}
**Selection criteria for Figure 2.** We keep only recovered original/replication effect pairs where both sides have a usable common-metric effect size on our shared $D$ axis, both sample sizes are known and at least 10, and the replication attempt is larger than the original ($N_r > N_o$). We also collapse obvious site-level repeats to one row per original paper × replication study × endpoint anchor, using summed replication $N$ and an $N$-weighted replication $D$. The unit here is therefore a paper-endpoint pair, not a paper: the current 1,451-row table comes from 821 unique original papers, and 236 of those originals contribute more than one row. That many-to-one structure is expected because one original paper can contribute several claims or outcomes, and some claims are re-tested by multiple labs or samples.
:::

The originals are not approximations to the truth that better statistics could clean up. They are filter artifacts: numbers the publication boundary selected into existence. There's nothing reanalysis can do. The only remedy is to rerun at large *N*.

When you read the next small-*N* paper claiming *d* = 0.8 from *N* = 40, this is what you're looking at.

## Most Effect Sizes Must be Small

The right question is not whether a published estimate crossed *p* < .05. The right question is: given what real effects usually look like, and given that this estimate survived a publication process that rewards large significant estimates, what should I now believe about the true effect?

The first input to that judgment is empirical, not philosophical. **@fig-replication-combined** gives us 1,451 recovered original/replication endpoint pairs, all on a common $D$ scale, all with both sample sizes known, and all with the replication larger than the original. In that sample, 81% of effects shrink, 42% shrink below the original *p* < .05 boundary at the replication sample size, and the fitted log-log relationship implies about 53% lower $D$ for each 10x increase in $N$. That distribution is not an oracle, but it is the least-filtered empirical prior available here: larger samples, direct retests, and far less room for the original publication boundary to select the observed magnitude.

The second input is structural. Appendix F lays out eleven reasons most effects must be small before we ever look at a *p*-value. The short version is that behavioral, biomedical, educational, and social outcomes are bounded noisy aggregates. They are produced by many causes, mediated through partial mechanisms, measured with finite reliability, diluted by active controls and counterfactual exposure, averaged across heterogeneous people and settings, and eroded by site-to-site voltage drop and scale-up decay [@tosh2024; @pearl2001mediation; @mackinnon2002mediation; @spearman1904; @hedge2018reliability; @bryan2021heterogeneity; @klein2018; @alubaydli2017scalability; @pereira2012largeeffects]. Ordinary interventions usually push on one weak edge in a dense causal graph, not on a single unconstrained bottleneck.

The third input is statistical. A published estimate is not just a noisy draw from the world. It is a noisy draw that survived a significance filter. Gelman and Carlin's Type M framework, van Zwet and Cator's significance-filter analysis, and the broader winner's-curse literature all imply the same direction of correction: conditional on significance, small noisy studies exaggerate magnitude [@gelman2014beyondpower; @zwet2021; @ioannidis2008inflation; @loken2017measurement]. So a rational reader needs shrinkage, not just standard errors.

That is what **@fig-prior-comparison** is for. The flat line is the implicit no-shrinkage prior: every effect from $d = 0.01$ to $d = 1,000$ treated as no less plausible than every other. The blue curve is the weakly informative Normal$(0, 0.35)$ prior. It does not ban large effects. It says they are rare enough to pay an evidentiary tax. About 95% of that prior lies inside $|d| < 0.69$. That is a reasonable public-facing approximation to what the replication distribution is already telling us.

![What you implicitly believe before the data. The flat/no-shrinkage line treats large effects as no less plausible than small effects; the Normal(0, 0.35) prior puts most mass on behavioral-science effects near zero.](_generated/figures/figure4_prior_comparison.png){#fig-prior-comparison}

Write the update the simple way:

$$
d \sim N(0, \tau^2), \qquad \hat d \mid d, s \sim N(d, s^2)
$$

$$
E[d \mid \hat d, s] = \frac{\tau^2}{\tau^2 + s^2} \hat d
$$

with $\tau = 0.35$. For a balanced two-group design, a useful approximation is $s \approx 2 / \sqrt{N}$. That makes the reader-facing lesson immediate: large studies have small $s$, so the data dominate; small studies have large $s$, so the estimate shrinks hard toward zero. The prior does not make large effects impossible. It makes small-$N$ large effects earn their keep.

Take the kind of result this essay keeps attacking: a 40-person study reporting $\hat d = 0.80$. Then $s \approx 2 / \sqrt{40} \approx 0.316$. Under the Normal$(0, 0.35)$ prior, the shrinkage weight is

$$
w = \frac{0.35^2}{0.35^2 + 0.316^2} \approx 0.55
$$

so the posterior mean is not $0.80$. It is about $0.44$. Run the same observed estimate at $N = 400$, where $s \approx 0.10$, and the weight rises to about 0.92, giving a posterior mean of about $0.74$. That is the whole argument in one contrast. The prior is not saying “big effects never happen.” It is saying “a huge effect from a tiny study is mostly evidence about noise plus selection unless the underlying causal story is extraordinary.”

And that is exactly what the replication evidence shows. Small-$N$ literatures produce large selected estimates; larger replication attempts pull them back toward the effect-size range the world actually allows. Our Figure 2 estimate --- 81% shrinking, 42% shrinking below the original significance boundary, and about 53% lower $D$ per 10x increase in $N$ --- is not a side fact. It is the empirical prior the rest of the paper is asking the reader to use.

Real large effects still exist. But when they are real, they look like vaccines, antibiotics, or other interventions whose targets visibly collapse under coarse measurement and large-scale replication, not like vague primes in 42 undergraduates. That is why the burden of evidence rises with the size of the claim. If a result is supposed to live in the thin tail of @fig-prior-comparison, then the evidence has to look tail-worthy too: large samples, hard outcomes, active controls, repeated success across sites, or effects obvious enough to see without statistical heroics.

## A World In Which Most Published Research Was True Would Be A Nightmare Hellscape {#imagine-life-if-you-could-believe-small-n-work}

Consider the following proof by contradiction. Suppose large effect sizes didn't require large evidence. Published research was mostly true and could be taken literally. What would that world look like? A 40-person undergraduate study reporting *d* = 0.8 would be as informative about reality as a 40,000-person preregistered RCT reporting the same thing. The sample size would be a cost consideration, not an epistemic one.

What would the published record look like in that world? @fig-published-d-vs-n now uses the expanded local published-focal corpus: 10,076 published-original candidate papers across 25 source corpora, each collapsed to a paper-level median D and N. The point cloud is no longer just the older statcheck/economics draft example; it is the current cross-field Codex build.

![The small-N and huge-effect-size pattern in published journal articles. The current paper-level corpus has 10,076 published-original candidate papers across 25 source corpora; median D is 0.41, median N is 164, and 83% sit above the p <= .10 boundary.](_generated/figures/figure3_published_d_vs_n.png){#fig-published-d-vs-n}

A frequentist defender would object here. A p \< 0.05 finding is not claimed with certainty; the threshold itself admits a 1-in-20 false-positive rate, and p \< 0.01 admits 1-in-100. Discount the current 10,076-paper published-focal corpus by those rates and the basic arithmetic barely changes: roughly 9,572 papers remain under a 5% discount and roughly 9,975 under a 1% discount. More importantly, the p-value discount is about whether an effect exists under the null, not whether the reported magnitude is right.

Take those papers at face value. Every point above the curve is a real large effect. That's roughly 12,000 significant psychology findings in this corpus alone, most of them reporting *d* between 0.3 and 2.0, from studies with 30 to 200 participants. Each describes an intervention --- a prime, a scale, a framing, a brief instruction --- that moves a human outcome by a substantial fraction of a standard deviation.

Multiply it out. If every published finding in this corpus were a real large effect, behavioral science would have discovered tens of thousands of vaccine-scale interventions on human cognition, judgment, and behavior over three decades. A typical psychology graduate program would be producing several such discoveries per year. The world would be rebuilt by them.

It isn't. None of these interventions has transformed a classroom, a clinic, a marketplace, or a voting booth at the magnitudes claimed. The claimed effects don't show up at scale, don't replicate under preregistration, don't survive contact with active controls. The published record is consistent with a world in which large effect sizes are common and easy to find. The actual world is not that one. So large effect sizes must require large evidence --- more than a 40-person study can produce.

## Because Large Effects Are Special, They Require Special Evidence {#large-effects-are-special}

The point is not that large effects never happen. It is that real large effects look like vaccines, antibiotics, and other interventions whose targets visibly collapse under coarse measurement and large-scale replication, not like vague primes in 42 undergraduates.

That is why the burden of evidence rises with the size of the claim. If a result is supposed to live in the thin tail of @fig-prior-comparison, then the evidence has to look tail-worthy too: large samples, hard outcomes, active controls, repeated success across sites, or effects obvious enough to see without statistical heroics.

## A world where everything mattered would be terrifying

That life is one in which your fate is dictated by trivia, most of it outside your control. A warm cup, a stray smell, an overhead bulb --- each a lever big enough to swing trust, honesty, testosterone, or the odds of a heart attack. Keeping a marriage, a job, or a working cardiovascular system would be a daily gauntlet: meticulous planning to control the inputs, and persistent luck when you couldn't. No equilibrium you built over years of practice, effort, or cooperation would be stable, because any small thing could knock it over. Choosing the wrong shirt could cost you the promotion. Sitting in the wrong chair could cost you the deal. The right cup of coffee could save your marriage. The wrong one could end it.

And if every small thing mattered, then in another sense nothing would. You couldn't decide what was worth your attention, because the list of things with life-sized consequences would be infinite. You couldn't learn from experience, because each day's outcome would be dominated by a different lurking confounder. You couldn't give advice to your children, because whatever rule you landed on would be overturned by the next trivial intervention to come down the pike. A world in which everything is a huge treatment effect is a world in which agency, planning, and accumulated knowledge collapse into noise.

## Mysticisms about extreme agency and helplessness are common throughout human culture

Humans keep inventing this worldview. In every era, in every culture, some version of it shows up: your fate is dictated by forces outside your control --- the stars, the gods, karma, ancestors, trauma, your microbiome, your vibration --- and also every small thing you do is loaded with ultimate consequence [@npr2022astrology]. You are simultaneously helpless and supernaturally responsible, and the contradiction is the point; a doctrine that resolved cleanly into either fatalism or agency would lose half its customers. The meme is stable because it pays two constituencies at once. For the individual it is the most flattering theory of the self ever invented: when things go well, you worked the system; when things go badly, the system (or karma [@lerner1980just], or someone else's negative energy) worked you. Vague enough personality claims feel personally true to almost everyone [@forer1949]. For predatory institutions the same structure is a revenue model, because if every small thing matters and the mechanisms are invisible, the list of fixes someone can sell you is infinite. Religions sold indulgences on this logic for centuries [@bossy1985christianity]; prosperity preachers sell seed-faith donations on it now [@bowler2013blessed]; a \$6.8-trillion global wellness industry sells supplements, cleanses, and energy work [@gwi2023wellness]; MLM uplines sell the dream that the right mindset produces the right income, and most participants make little or no money doing it [@ftc2018mlm]. The product varies; the sales pitch is identical --- *there is a hidden lever, you have been pulling it wrong, and I can show you how.* The pitch only works in a world of huge treatment effects. In a world of small, noisy, hard-won effects, no one needs a guru.

## Science's version of this mysticism is small-*N* frequentism (a cult of the naive and the criminally negligent) {#sciences-version-of-this-meme-is-small-n-studies-with-tiny-interventions-and-huge-effect-sizes}

The published scientific literature has its own version of the same meme. A graduate student runs 42 undergraduates through a two-minute posture manipulation and reports that the treatment raised testosterone by 30% and risk tolerance by 45%. A lab runs 67 undergraduates past a plate of radishes and reports that willpower is a depletable resource that cookies consume. A paper runs 30 students through a scrambled-sentence prime and reports that reading words about old age makes people walk 15% slower down a hallway. Each study has a tiny sample, a tiny intervention, and a huge reported effect. Each was published in a prestigious journal. Each produced a decade of follow-up research, popular-press coverage, TED talks, management consulting, and policy recommendations. The structure is the same as the religious version: a small, nearly invisible lever produces an enormous, life-sized consequence. The statistics are different from magic, but the shape of the claim is not.

### What a flat prior commits you to

Strictly speaking, NHST does not have a prior. That is part of the problem. A *p*-value is not (P(H_0 \| data)), and a confidence interval is not born as a posterior distribution over the true effect. But when readers convert a significant estimate and its confidence interval into belief about the true effect, the object they are tacitly using is the flat-prior posterior in disguise. In the normal approximation, δ̂ \| δ ∼ *N*(δ, *s*²); combine that likelihood with the improper prior π(δ) ∝ 1 and the posterior is δ \| δ̂ ∼ *N*(δ̂, *s*²) --- the same Gaussian bell that the confidence distribution traces. That is the no-shrinkage prior: not a probability distribution, not normalizable, and perfectly happy to assign equal density to equal-width intervals around *d* = 0.2, *d* = 5, and *d* = 1,000. No one believes this out loud. **@fig-prior-comparison** draws what the belief would look like. The publication system behaves as if authors and reviewers were doing exactly that calculation.

NHST contains no prior at all. The flat prior appears when readers read a significant estimate and its confidence interval as a belief distribution over the true effect---which is how unshrunk point estimates routinely get read. With that implicit flat prior, a significant *p*-value at *n* = 30 is treated as evidence for an effect. A reasonable prior --- one that puts, say, 95% of its mass in \|*d*\| \< 0.7, because that's where psychology-sized effects actually live --- would demand far more data before allowing the same conclusion. The flat prior removes this brake, and the consequence is the gamification of discovery: instead of asking "how much data do I need to detect a psychologically plausible effect," a researcher asks "how little data can I get away with and still clear the threshold." The answer is often remarkably little --- thirty subjects, if the effect you report is big enough. Coupled with the publication filter, where journals print the papers that clear *p* \< 0.05 and not the ones that don't, you end up with a literature that consists almost entirely of studies that had to report implausibly large effects in order to exist, and almost none that reported the actual small effects that were there. The published record contains the untrue things and excludes the true ones. @fig-publication-boundary and @fig-prior-comparison together are the mechanism. @fig-published-d-distribution is what the mechanism produces.

![The current published-original candidate corpus as a distribution of paper-level effect sizes. 76% of papers have median D >= 0.2, 41% have median D >= 0.5, and 21% have median D >= 0.8.](_generated/figures/figure5_published_d_distribution.png){#fig-published-d-distribution}

### What the published distribution actually looks like {#what-the-published-distribution-looks-like}

If you believe any one of the 10,076 papers in the current published-corpus figure is a literal reading of the effect size in the world, you have to believe the process that produced the whole cloud.

## A Sane Belief System that Matches Our Actual World {#what-we-should-actually-believe-about-effect-sizes}

By "small-*N* studies don't matter" I mean the thing that deserves contempt: underpowered, one-shot, between-person studies of noisy behavioral, biomedical, or social outcomes, especially when they report large effects from tiny interventions. I do not mean within-subject psychophysics where one subject contributes thousands of trials, single-gene knockouts in model organisms, or any setting where the signal-to-noise ratio is high enough that *N* = 10 has real resolving power. Those are not the cult. The cult is 42 people, one weird prime, one *p*-value, and a civilization-scale conclusion. The rest of this essay is about the cult.

The solution is not to work harder at running small-*N* studies. It is to change what you believe before you look at the data. The frequentist prior in @fig-prior-comparison is what makes @fig-published-d-distribution possible. A prior that reflects the world we actually live in --- a world where real effects are usually small, where large effects are rare and usually restricted to interventions that are obvious without a study --- shrinks small-*N* findings automatically, before any question of *p*-value or publication arises.

The Gelman prior is a reasonable place to start --- reasonable in the literal sense, that a reasonable person, told in advance what behavioral-science effect sizes look like, would write down something approximately like Normal(0, 0.35). The replication evidence shown earlier (@fig-replication-combined) is where this prior comes from empirically: the post-replication distribution of effect sizes sits almost exactly where the Gelman prior said it would.

## Objections

*Four objections are worth taking seriously. None of them saves the literature this essay is about.*

### "But there are exceptions where effects really are large." {#obj-exceptions}

Appendix B catalogs the exceptions in detail. The taxonomy is not alphabetical and not by academic field; it is by architectural type. Infectious disease control. Deficiency replacement. Mechanical and structural interventions. A handful of cardiovascular and metabolic pharmacologic classes. A short list of psychiatric interventions with clear mechanistic targets. A few educational interventions that happen to combine intensity, targeting, and a weak counterfactual. Behavioral policy interventions that change defaults rather than minds. Agricultural and environmental interventions that address a genuine limiting factor.

What these share is the shape of the compound model with each factor pushed favorably. A dominant causal bottleneck means *k* is effectively 1, so 1/√*k* is not small. A short causal chain means the product of path coefficients is close to 1. A large counterfactual gap means the control arm's shared-ingredient fraction is close to 0. A well-measured proximal outcome means *r~xx~* is close to 1. When all four structural factors are favorable and the intervention is tested rigorously enough that the selection filter does no work, the effect can be enormous. Antibiotics against susceptible pathogens produce *d*-equivalents that do not fit on normal charts. Insulin moved life expectancy in Type 1 diabetes from months to decades. Cataract surgery restores sight.

The pattern of exceptions is the strongest possible confirmation of the rule. Large effects require specific structural conditions. Those conditions are rare. Their rarity is why most effects are small. Interventions that meet the full checklist are not distributed uniformly across human problems; they cluster in a few therapeutic areas (infection, deficiency, mechanical dysfunction) and a few behavioral domains (default architecture, choice of instruction intensity). Outside those clusters, the expected effect of any intervention is small, and researchers who report large effects should be read as having either (a) found a rare genuine exception that satisfies the checklist, or (b) produced an estimate that will not survive contact with replication, scale-up, or bias correction.

### "It's just p-hacking. Fix the filter and the rest takes care of itself." {#obj-phacking}

Fix every form of p-hacking tomorrow. A two-arm behavioral study with *N* = 40 still crosses *p* \< 0.05 on an observed *d* of roughly 0.62, still has 23% power against a true *d* = 0.4, still selects upper-tail draws from its sampling distribution. Type M inflation on a small-*N* significant finding is approximately 2× under fully honest analysis---a property of the sampling distribution, not of researcher behavior.

Registered Reports make this concrete. Scheel, Schijen, and Lakens [@scheel2021] compared 71 preregistered Registered Reports to 152 standard articles and found positive-result rates of 96% versus 44%---a 52 percentage-point drop once p-hacking is removed by design. But 44% of honestly-analyzed studies still cross significance, in a world where most true effects are under *d* = 0.25. Those positive findings are inflated upper-tail draws. They still don't replicate cleanly.

The filter is not the primary disease. The primary disease is that the resolution of the instrument doesn't match the size of the object being measured. Remove p-hacking and you trade a literature of inflated positives for a literature of honest small-sample noise plus the rare large effect. Cleaner, but not informative.

### "Researchers calibrate sample size to the true effect." {#obj-calibration}

This is the anthropic principle: investigators have priors about effect sizes, run power analyses, and size their *N* accordingly. A published *N* = 40 reflects an investigator who had reason to expect a detectable effect at that sample.

The best version of this objection is van Zwet, Gelman, and Więcek [@vanzwet2026], whose model of 80,000 studies across 11 curated corpora includes exactly this claim. Their data refute it.

Across their corpora---Cochrane, clinical trials, ecology, political science, exercise research, economics, psychology, psychotherapy, education, intelligence---**3--11% of studies achieve 80% probability of significance**. Not eighty percent of studies. Three to eleven. Political science: 7%. Cochrane: 10%. Exercise and cognition: 3%. The modal published study is radically underpowered for the effect it is nominally detecting. vZGW's own interpretation is that researchers size studies "just about large enough to assess the direction of the effect of interest, but not larger"---under pressures of time, money, and logistics.

That is not calibration. It is the minimum sample required to cross the filter on a favorable noise draw, which is exactly what the rest of this essay has argued. The anthropic principle is real; it does not say what the objection needs it to say. It says researchers size for filter-crossing, not for detection.

### "At least the sign is right." {#obj-sign}

The sharpest version of this objection is vZGW [@vanzwet2026]. Their model reports that published significant findings carry the correct sign 82--96% of the time at \|*z*\| = 1.96 and 94--99% at \|*z*\| ≥ 1.96, across all 11 of their curated corpora. Their interpretation: *"empirical research often leads to true findings---in the sense of correctly identifying the direction of an effect---that do not replicate in the sense of achieving statistically significant result in the same direction as the original study."* Magnitudes are inflated; direction is informative; the replication crisis is overstated.

This is wrong, and the way it is wrong is instructive.

**The numbers are not a measurement.** They are the output of a model. vZGW fit a mixture of half-normals to the observed \|*z*\| distribution in each corpus, apply a Hedges single-parameter correction for publication bias, deconvolve out a standard normal noise component, and read off a posterior distribution over the true SNR. The 82--96% correct-sign figure is what that posterior implies under the model's assumptions. It is not the result of comparing published signs to any external ground truth.

**The model's central assumption fails in exactly the subfields where the crisis lives.** The assumption is that observed *z* equals true SNR plus standard normal noise. For this to hold, the published estimate must be an unbiased draw from its sampling distribution. That assumption holds when the study is well-identified: the construct is measured cleanly, the intervention is delivered as described, selection into the sample is exogenous, the analysis is fixed in advance. In that regime, upper-tail draws that cross the filter are disproportionately sign-concordant with the true effect, because the true mean and forward-direction noise point the same way. Correct sign 90% of the time falls out.

The assumption does not hold when the research process itself has degrees of freedom correlated with direction---specification selection, construct misspecification, demand characteristics, identification strategies that don't identify what they claim. In that regime, the "noise" is not symmetric around the true effect. It is drawn from the space of specifications the researcher was willing to report. The filter still selects upper-tail draws, but those draws align with **what the researcher expected to find**, not with reality. A field that operates on a wrong shared prior produces a literature full of findings pointing in the theory-predicted direction, regardless of whether the theory is correct. vZGW's model reads the resulting internal consistency as "high correct-sign rate." It is not. It is internal consistency with a wrong prior.

**The direct empirical test defeats the model where it matters.** vZGW's model does not have access to the true sign of the effect. Large preregistered replications do, at least approximately, because they restore the conditions under which observed *z* really is an unbiased draw. Run the test:

-   Chabris et al. 2012 [@chabris2012]: 12 previously-published candidate-IQ-gene effects tested in roughly 10,000 subjects. Replicated: **zero**. A coin produces six. Published sign correct rate: 0%.
-   Duncan and Keller 2011 [@duncan2011]: 103 novel psychiatric G×E findings, 10 direct replications, 27% replication rate, pair-level sign match near 50%. Coin-equivalent.
-   The Registered Replication Report program and Many Labs 3: ego depletion, facial feedback, professor priming, elderly priming, warmth priming---in aggregate, sign concordance with originals in the 40--60% range.

In the subfields where the replication crisis actually landed---candidate genes, social priming, much of classical social psychology, the first decade of behavioral genetics---the empirical sign concordance between original small-*N* findings and large-*N* replications is at or below chance. vZGW's model predicts 82--96%. Reality delivers 0--50%.

**This is not a "different subfield" problem.** It is a model-specification problem. vZGW's framework cannot distinguish a literature that gets the sign right 90% of the time from a literature that is internally consistent 90% of the time in the direction of a wrong theory. Their model assumes the first; their data are compatible with either; the fields where the assumption is testable show the second. Their optimism is what their model outputs when their assumption is satisfied. In the fields where that optimism would actually matter, the assumption fails.

The replication crisis is not about magnitudes being imprecise while directions remain true. It is about the research process in specific subfields producing published literatures that do not carry reliable information about reality---not about magnitude, not about sign. vZGW have described a mathematical property of their model. They have not described the literature.

## What this means for reading research

Carry the empirical distribution into every paper. When a behavioral or social science study reports *d* \> 0.5, that estimate sits above the 90th percentile of its field's real distribution. It is not obviously wrong, but it is not obviously right either. Ask whether the intervention plausibly satisfies enough of the structural checklist to justify its position in the tail. Ask whether the sample was large enough for the estimate to be informative about anything other than the sampling distribution. Ask whether the outcome was pre-specified. Ask whether the comparator was a placebo or something closer to active care. Ask whether the study's authors have a credible record of similar-magnitude effects surviving replication.

When an observational study in medicine or epidemiology reports an odds ratio of 3 or a hazard ratio of 0.4, default to skepticism. Those magnitudes are rare in the experimental literature and common in the confounded one. The class of claims large enough to be clinically transformative but small enough to be within observational reach is narrow; most claims in that range are confounded, and Mendelian randomization, instrumental variables, or RCTs where available tend to attenuate or reverse them.

Small, precisely estimated effects from large preregistered studies with active controls are more credible than large, imprecisely estimated effects from small exploratory ones --- and they are more predictive of real-world impact when interventions are scaled. The question to carry into every paper is not "is this significant?" but "is this effect size plausible given the empirical distribution of effects in this field, the structural features of this intervention, and the methodological strength of this study?"

## What this means for doing research

Design studies for the effect sizes that exist. A study powered to detect *d* = 0.50 in a field whose real effects cluster at 0.15 is powered to detect only the most inflated claims, and will --- conditional on producing a significant result --- contribute mostly to the right tail of the literature rather than to its center. Sample sizes must match the empirical distribution, which means they must often be larger than current norms by one to two orders of magnitude.

Preregister the primary analysis. Use active controls where the counterfactual is otherwise ill-defined. Measure outcomes reliably enough that the *r~xx~* ceiling is not the binding constraint. Report bias-corrected estimates alongside naive ones when meta-analyzing. Treat a surprising effect size as a prompt to investigate the DGP rather than to celebrate the finding. None of this is methodological luxury; it is the minimum required to contribute signal rather than noise to a literature that already has too much of the latter.

The deepest implication of the small-effects world is that most research, honestly done, will produce small effects, and that most interventions, honestly scaled, will produce small average changes on the outcomes they target. This is not an indictment of research or of intervention. Small effects, applied at scale across populations and compounded across exposures, are how most of the genuine progress in public health, education, and development has been made. Blood pressure lowered by 2 mmHg prevents thousands of strokes. A few-percent reduction in smoking compounds to enormous lifetime mortality differences. A small per-year effect of a good teacher compounds to hundreds of thousands of dollars in lifetime earnings across a classroom of students. Small *d* is not small consequence. It is the actual scale at which real interventions on real outcomes operate, once the filter is removed and the counterfactual is honestly specified.

Knowing this changes what you are looking for when you read, what you are willing to publish when you write, and what you expect when you plan. It is not a posture of skepticism. It is the default prior the empirical record demands.

## The real distribution --- effect sizes across science

This appendix compiles the quantitative evidence underlying the claim that most treatment effects are small. It draws on replication projects, meta-analyses of meta-analyses, government-scale RCT databases, Cochrane reviews, and meta-research studies of publication bias. Within each field, the organizing question is the same: what does the distribution of true effects actually look like, once we correct for the filters between reality and what ends up in print?

::: appendix-toc
**Appendix A --- The real distribution (you are here)** [A.1 Metrics](#a-overview)[·]{.sep} [A.2 Headline numbers](#a-headline)[·]{.sep} [A.3 Psychology](#a-psych)[·]{.sep} [A.4 Medicine](#a-med)[·]{.sep} [A.5 Surgery](#a-surg)[·]{.sep} [A.6 Alt medicine](#a-alt)[·]{.sep} [A.7 Mental health](#a-mh)[·]{.sep} [A.8 Epidemiology](#a-epi)[·]{.sep} [A.9 Genetics](#a-gen)[·]{.sep} [A.10 Neuroscience](#a-neuro)[·]{.sep} [A.11 Economics](#a-econ)[·]{.sep} [A.12 Development econ](#a-dev)[·]{.sep} [A.13 Education](#a-edu)[·]{.sep} [A.14 Political science](#a-poli)[·]{.sep} [A.15 Criminology](#a-crim)[·]{.sep} [A.16 Sociology](#a-soc)[·]{.sep} [A.17 Organizational](#a-org)[·]{.sep} [A.18 Marketing](#a-mkt)[·]{.sep} [A.19 Environmental](#a-env)[·]{.sep} [A.20 Sports science](#a-sport)[·]{.sep} [A.21 Agriculture](#a-ag)[·]{.sep} [A.22 COVID-19](#a-covid)[·]{.sep} [A.23 Publication bias](#a-pubbias)[·]{.sep} [A.24 Many-analysts](#a-many)[·]{.sep} [A.25 Obs→exp reversals](#a-obs-exp)[·]{.sep} [A.26 WEIRD samples](#a-weird)[·]{.sep} [A.27 Time trajectory](#a-time) **Appendix B --- When the world gives you large effects** [B.1 Infectious disease](#b-infectious)[·]{.sep} [B.2 Deficiency replacement](#b-replacement)[·]{.sep} [B.3 Mechanical/structural](#b-mechanical)[·]{.sep} [B.4 Cardiovascular](#b-cv)[·]{.sep} [B.5 Mental health exceptions](#b-mh)[·]{.sep} [B.6 Education exceptions](#b-edu)[·]{.sep} [B.7 Policy architecture](#b-policy)[·]{.sep} [B.8 Agriculture](#b-ag)[·]{.sep} [B.9 Management](#b-mgmt)[·]{.sep} [B.10 What exceptions share](#b-pattern) **Appendix C --- The hellscape catalog** [C.1 Social priming](#c-priming)[·]{.sep} [C.2 Self-regulation](#c-depletion)[·]{.sep} [C.3 Stereotype effects](#c-stereo)[·]{.sep} [C.4 Candidate genes](#c-genes)
:::

### A.1   Metrics and what counts as "small" {#a-overview}

Most of what follows uses three metrics. **Cohen's *d*** (and its near-equivalents Hedges' *g*, SMD) expresses a group difference in standard deviations. **Pearson's *r*** expresses an association as a correlation coefficient. For small effects, *r* ≈ *d*/2; so *d* = 0.20 corresponds to *r* ≈ 0.10. For binary outcomes, **odds ratios**, **risk ratios**, and **hazard ratios** express multiplicative effects on event rates.

Cohen's original benchmarks --- *d* = 0.20 small, 0.50 medium, 0.80 large; *r* = 0.10 small, 0.30 medium, 0.50 large --- were proposed in 1988 as rough guides, not empirical claims. Four decades of meta-research have shown them to be systematically optimistic for most of social and behavioral science. Empirically calibrated benchmarks vary by field:

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 1. A.1 Metrics and what counts as “small”**

| Source | Field | Small (25th) | Medium (50th) | Large (75th) |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cohen [@cohen1988] | Proposed universally | r =0.10 | r =0.30 | r =0.50 |
| Gignac & Szodorai [@gignac2016] | Individual differences (708 meta-analytic r ’s) | r =0.11 | r =0.19 | r =0.29 |
| Lovakov & Agadullina [@lovakov2021] | Social psychology (12,170 r ’s, 134 meta-analyses) | r =0.12 | r =0.24 | r =0.41 |
| Funder & Ozer [@funder2019] | Psychology, recommended | r =0.05 | r =0.20 | r =0.30 |
| Doucouliagos [@doucouliagos2011] | Economics (22,000 partial r ’s) | 0.07 | 0.17 | 0.33 |
| Kraft [@kraft2020] | Education RCTs | d <0.05 | d =0.05–0.20 | d ≥0.20 |
:::

Cohen's "medium" of *r* = 0.30 is the 75th percentile of social psychology and above the 90th percentile of education RCTs. His "large" of *r* = 0.50 is functionally absent from published meta-analyses of interventions in most applied fields. When this appendix refers to an effect as "small," it means small relative to the empirical distribution in that field, not relative to Cohen's out-of-calibration benchmarks.

### A.2   Headline numbers, one row per field {#a-headline}

The table below is the executive summary of this appendix. Each row is a claim about the typical effect size in a field, with the source that supplies the number. Subsequent sections unpack each row.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 2. A.2 Headline numbers, one row per field**

| Field | Typical effect | Metric | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Social psychology | r = 0.24 (median) | correlation | Lovakov & Agadullina [@lovakov2021] |
| Psychology, replications | d → 0.15–0.20 | SMD after replication | OSC 2015; Many Labs 2; SSRP 2018; SCORE 2026 |
| Clinical medicine (all Cochrane RCTs) | median power 13% | typical exaggeration ≈ 3× | van Zwet, Schwab & Senn [@zwet2021] |
| Pharmacotherapy, all drugs vs. placebo | SMD = 0.37–0.41 | median Hedges’ g | Leucht et al. [@leucht2012] |
| Oncology drugs, median OS gain | 2.1 months | overall survival | Fojo et al. |
| Psychotherapy vs. no treatment | d = 0.70–0.80 | SMD | Wampold [@wampold2019] |
| Psychotherapy vs. active control | d = 0.20–0.30 | SMD | Cuijpers et al. [@cuijpers2018] |
| Brain-behavior (reproducible BWAS) | r = 0.01 | correlation | Marek et al. [@marek2022] |
| Individual genetic variants (GWAS) | <0.1% variance each | per-SNP R ² | Visscher et al.; Yengo et al. |
| Academic nudge studies | 8.7 pp | percentage points | DellaVigna & Linos [@dellavigna2022] |
| Same nudges at government scale | 1.4 pp (6× shrinkage) | percentage points | DellaVigna & Linos [@dellavigna2022] |
| Development interventions | d ≈ 0.12 | SMD | Vivalt [@vivalt2020] |
| Education RCTs | median d = 0.10; weighted mean 0.04 | SMD | Kraft [@kraft2020] |
| Campaign persuasion effects | ≈ 0 | percentage points | Kalla & Broockman [@kalla2018] |
| Hot-spots policing | d = 0.13 | SMD | Braga et al. [@braga2019] |
| Nutritional epidemiology (meta-analyses) | RR ≈ 0.96 | relative risk | Schoenfeld & Ioannidis [@schoenfeld2013] |
| Personnel selection (cognitive ability) | r = 0.31 | validity coefficient | Sackett et al. [@sackett2022] |
| Digital advertising on sales ( R ²) | ≈ 0.000005 | individual-level R ² | Lewis & Rao [@lewis2015] |
| Carbon pricing on emissions | −5 to −21% | % emissions | Döbbeling-Hildebrandt et al. [@d2024] |
| Nitrogen fertilizer on crop yield | +30 to +75% | % yield | Global meta-analyses |
| Insecticide-treated bednets on child mortality | RR = 0.83 (17% reduction) | relative risk | Cochrane |
:::

::: {#a-fields .part-label}
Part I --- Field by field
:::

### A.3   Psychology {#a-psych}

#### Empirical benchmarks across the whole field

Richard, Bond, and Stokes-Zoota [@richard2003] synthesized 322 meta-analyses covering one century of social psychology --- roughly 25,000 studies and 8 million participants --- and reported a mean effect of *r* = 0.21 with a median of 0.18. Fewer than 24% of effects reached Cohen's "medium" threshold of *r* = 0.30. Lovakov and Agadullina [@lovakov2021] updated this using 12,170 correlations from 134 meta-analyses, with 25th/50th/75th percentiles at 0.12/0.24/0.41. Gignac and Szodorai [@gignac2016], examining 708 individual-differences correlations, found percentiles at 0.11/0.19/0.29, with fewer than 3% of correlations reaching Cohen's "large" of *r* = 0.50. Funder and Ozer [@funder2019] argued that *r* ≥ 0.40 should be viewed with suspicion as likely inflated.

#### Replication projects

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 3. Replication projects**

| Project | Scope | Original | Replicated | Pass rate |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Open Science Collaboration [@collaboration2015] | 100 psych studies | r =0.403 | r =0.197 | 36% |
| Many Labs 2 [@klein2018] | 28 effects, 125 samples, N=15,305 | d =0.60 | d =0.15 | 50–54% |
| SSRP [@camerer2018] | 21 Nature/Science studies | — | ~50% of original | 62% |
| Exp. Economics RP [@camerer2016] | 18 AER/QJE lab studies | — | 66% of original | 61% |
| SCORE [@tyner2026] | 274 claims, 164 papers, 12 disciplines | r =0.25 | r =0.10 | 55% |
:::

#### Famous failed and severely shrunken effects

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 4. Famous failed and severely shrunken effects**

| Effect | Original | Replication | Shrinkage |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ego depletion | d =0.62 | d =0.04 (23 labs, N=2,141) | ~94% |
| Facial feedback (pen-in-mouth) | 0.82 units | 0.03 units (17 labs) | ~96% |
| Elderly-walking priming (Bargh) | d =0.82–1.08 | d ≈0 | ~100% |
| Power posing (hormonal effects) | significant | p >0.16 (Ranehill) | ~100% |
| Money priming | d =0.34–0.75 | d =−0.02 (36 labs) | ~100% |
| Warm cup → warm personality | r =0.28–0.31 | r ≈0 (N=861) | ~100% |
| Implicit bias (IAT) → behavior | r =0.274 | r =0.148 (Oswald meta) | ~46% |
| Growth mindset interventions | d =0.08 [@sisk2018] | d =0.02 (highest-quality, Macnamara 2023) | ~75% |
| Stereotype threat | d >0.5 | d =0.22–0.26 meta; possibly null after bias correction | 50–100% |
:::

::: {.callout .coral}
**Anchoring is the exception.** Numerical anchoring on judgments replicates consistently and at substantial magnitudes (*d* ≈ 0.4--0.8). It is the rare social priming effect that survives. Its survival may tell us something about the others: anchoring operates on an explicit numerical judgment, a short causal chain with a clean measurement. Most failed priming effects asked whether an incidental cue changed a distant behavior through an inferred cognitive route.
:::

#### Noncognitive skills: grit, mindset, deliberate practice

**Grit** (Credé, Tynan & Harms [@cred2017] (2017); 88 samples, N = 66,807): population-corrected correlation with academic performance is ρ = 0.18. Grit correlates ρ = 0.84 with conscientiousness, and after controlling for conscientiousness its incremental validity is β = −0.02 --- null.

**Growth mindset**: the correlation with achievement is *r* ≈ 0.09 (about 1% of variance). Intervention effects are *d* = 0.08 overall [@sisk2018], declining to *d* = 0.05 in Macnamara & Burgoyne [@macnamara2023] (63 studies, N = 97,672), and *d* = 0.02 in the six highest-quality studies. After publication-bias correction, effects are non-significant. Studies by authors with financial conflicts of interest reported significantly larger effects.

**Deliberate practice** (Macnamara, Hambrick & Oswald [@macnamara2014]; 88 studies): practice explains 26% of variance in games, 21% in music, 18% in sports, 4% in education, and less than 1% in professional performance. Among elite performers specifically, practice explains about 1%. Ericsson's "10,000 hours" substantially overstated the contribution.

#### Cognitive training and far transfer

Sala et al. [@sala2019] conducted a second-order meta-analysis across 233 comparisons spanning all cognitive training domains. Once placebo effects and publication bias were controlled, the overall far-transfer effect was zero, with zero between-study variance. Near transfer exists (*g* ≈ 0.37--0.72), but far transfer to fluid intelligence, academic achievement, or real-world performance is consistently null. Owen et al. [@owen2010] tested over 11,000 participants on online brain training and found no transfer.

#### Clinical psychology and psychotherapy

See section A.7.

### A.4   Medicine & pharmacology {#a-med}

#### The Cochrane baseline

Van Zwet, Schwab, and Senn [@zwet2021] analyzed 23,551 RCTs from the Cochrane Database and found median statistical power of only 13%. At this power level, significant results overestimate true effects by approximately a factor of 3; even a just-significant result (*p* = 0.05) overestimates by about 70%. About 88% of RCTs have power below 80%.

Leucht et al. [@leucht2012] compared drug efficacy across 94 meta-analyses and found median SMDs of 0.37 for general medicine and 0.41 for psychiatric drugs --- small by Cohen's benchmarks, and indistinguishable between fields. Leucht et al. [@leucht2015] subsequently found that only 11 of 17 commonly used medications demonstrated a minimal clinically important difference.

Pereira, Horwitz, and Ioannidis [@pereira2012] asked how often medical interventions produce very large effects (OR ≥ 5). Across 85,002 forest plots from 3,082 Cochrane reviews, roughly 10% of first-published trials reported very large effects, but these almost always came from small studies with fewer than 20 events. Only one intervention across the entire Cochrane database --- extracorporeal oxygenation for neonates --- demonstrated a very large effect on mortality with high-quality supporting evidence.

#### Drug-class summary

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 5. Drug-class summary**

| Intervention | Comparator | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Antidepressants (21 drugs) | Placebo | SMD=0.30 | Cipriani et al. [@cipriani2018] (522 RCTs, N=116,477) |
| Antidepressants, FDA vs. published | — | Published inflated ~32% | Turner et al. [@turner2008] |
| Antipsychotics (schizophrenia) | Placebo | SMD=0.47; NNT=5 for minimal response | Huhn et al. [@huhn2019] ; Leucht |
| Anxiolytics (SSRI/SNRI/benzo, GAD) | Placebo | g =0.37 | Bandelow et al. |
| Methylphenidate (ADHD core symptoms) | Placebo | SMD=−0.78 (large) | Multiple meta-analyses |
| Paracetamol (back pain) | Placebo | Zero | Machado et al. [@machado2015] |
| NSAIDs (spinal pain) | Placebo | Clinically unimportant | Machado et al. [@machado2017] |
| Ibuprofen 400mg (acute pain) | Placebo | NNT=2.5 | Moore et al. |
| Statins (primary prev., low risk) | Placebo | NNT=167 over 5 years | CTT Collaboration |
| Statins (secondary prev.) | Placebo | NNT=19–21 over 10 years | CTT Collaboration |
| Antihypertensives, per 10 mmHg SBP | — | RR=0.80 for CVD events | Ettehad et al. [@ettehad2016] |
| SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) | Placebo | HR=0.62 CV death | EMPA-REG OUTCOME |
| GLP-1 agonists (liraglutide) | Placebo | HR=0.87 MACE | LEADER trial |
| Oncology drugs (median OS gain) | Control | 2.1 months | Fojo et al. |
| Cancer drugs at EMA approval | — | 57% lacked evidence of OS/QoL benefit | Davis et al. [@davis2017] |
| Nicotine replacement therapy | Placebo | OR=1.37–1.41 for quit | Cochrane |
| Varenicline | Placebo | OR=2.33 for quit | Cochrane |
| Naltrexone (alcohol, heavy drinking) | Placebo | NNT=12 | Jonas et al. [@jonas2014] |
| Opioid agonist treatment (mortality) | Out of treatment | Rate ratio ~3.2× lower | Sordo et al. [@sordo2017] |
:::

::: {.callout .blue}
**Preclinical to clinical.** Sena et al. [@sena2010] showed publication bias inflates preclinical animal stroke effects by \~31%. The Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology [@errington2021] replicated 50 experiments and found replication effects 85% smaller on average, with 40% replication rate. Roughly 5% of animal-tested therapeutic interventions obtain human regulatory approval [@ineichen2024] --- a useful anchor for the magnitude of the preclinical-to-clinical translation gap.
:::

### A.5   Surgery & procedures {#a-surg}

Sham-controlled surgical trials have produced some of modern medicine's most striking null findings. Jonas et al. [@jonas2015] meta-analyzed 39 studies comparing real surgery to sham procedures and found an overall SMD of 0.34, falling to 0.13 (non-significant) for pain-related conditions. Sham-surgery patients achieved 78% of the improvement seen in active surgery groups for pain outcomes.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 6. A.5 Surgery & procedures**

| Procedure | Trial | Finding |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Arthroscopic knee surgery (OA) | Moseley et al. [@moseley2002] , N=180 | Zero vs. sham |
| Arthroscopic partial meniscectomy | Sihvonen et al. (FIDELITY) 2013, N=146 | Zero vs. sham at 2y and 5y |
| PCI for stable angina | ORBITA 2018, N=200 | +16.6 s exercise time (NS) |
| PCI monotherapy for stable angina | ORBITA-2 2023, N=301 | +59.5 s — about 1 antianginal med |
| Invasive vs. conservative strategy, stable CAD | ISCHEMIA 2020, N=5,179 | No difference in composite CV outcome |
| Vertebroplasty (spinal fractures) | Multiple blinded RCTs | Null vs. sham |
:::

A review of 53 sham-controlled surgical trials found sham surgery as effective as real surgery in more than half. Because most surgical trials cannot be blinded, the published surgical literature is systematically inflated relative to what properly controlled designs reveal.

### A.6   Alternative & complementary medicine {#a-alt}

**Acupuncture** (Vickers et al. individual patient meta-analysis, 39 RCTs, 20,827 patients): acupuncture vs. sham acupuncture shows SMDs of 0.15--0.23; acupuncture vs. no treatment shows 0.42--0.57. About 75% of the total effect is attributable to non-specific (placebo) factors; roughly 25% is the "specific" needling effect.

**Homeopathy** (Shang et al. [@shang2005], 110 trials): the eight largest, highest-quality trials yielded OR = 0.88 (95% CI 0.66--1.18, *p* = 0.41) --- non-significant. Australia's NHMRC [@nhmrc2015] concluded there is no good-quality evidence homeopathy is effective for any health condition.

**Chiropractic / spinal manipulation** for chronic low back pain: SMT vs. recommended therapies produces a pain reduction of −5.63 points on a 0--100 scale --- statistically significant, clinically trivial.

### A.7   Mental health treatments {#a-mh}

Effect sizes in psychotherapy research depend critically on the comparison group.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 7. A.7 Mental health treatments**

| Intervention | Comparison | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Psychotherapy (general, depression) | Waitlist / no treatment | d =0.70–0.80 | Smith & Glass [@smith1977] ; Wampold 2019 |
| Psychotherapy (depression) | Active placebo | d =0.20–0.30 | Cuijpers et al. [@cuijpers2018] |
| Bona fide therapy A vs. bona fide therapy B | — | d =0.00–0.20 (“Dodo bird”) | Luborsky; Wampold |
| CBT for anxiety | Waitlist | d =1.23 | Carpenter et al. [@carpenter2018] |
| CBT for anxiety | Placebo | g =0.56 (2008–2018); 0.24 (2017–2022) | Carpenter et al. |
| Mindfulness-based programs | Active controls | SMD=0.30–0.38 | Goyal et al. [@goyal2014] (JAMA) |
| Trauma-focused CBT, EMDR (PTSD) | Waitlist | d ≈1.0+; NNT<4 | Cusack et al. [@cusack2016] |
| CBT-I (insomnia) | Control | g =0.57–0.98 | van Straten et al. [@straten2017] (2017) |
:::

::: {.callout .purple}
**The waitlist inflation problem.** Waitlist controls typically show *negative* change over time as symptomatic clients wait for help they know is coming; this inflates the apparent effect of any intervention compared against them. Active placebo and treatment-as-usual comparisons, which are more honest counterfactuals, routinely yield effects half the size or smaller. Almost every flagship psychotherapy finding collapses by about 50--70% when the comparator moves from waitlist to active control.
:::

### A.8   Epidemiology & nutrition {#a-epi}

Nutritional epidemiology is the field where the gap between individual studies and meta-analyses is largest. Schoenfeld and Ioannidis [@schoenfeld2013] found that 80% of common cookbook ingredients had been linked to cancer in at least one study. Individual studies claimed median relative risks of 2.20 (increased-risk claims) or 0.52 (decreased-risk claims). Meta-analyses of the same associations yielded a median RR of 0.96, with interquartile range 0.85--1.10 --- essentially null.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 8. A.8 Epidemiology & nutrition**

| Exposure | Outcome | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Smoking | Lung cancer | RR≈15–30 | Established epidemiology |
| Mediterranean diet | Composite CVD | HR=0.70 | PREDIMED |
| Mediterranean diet | All-cause mortality | HR=1.0 (null) | PREDIMED |
| Processed meat (per 50g/day) | Colorectal cancer | RR=1.18 | IARC meta |
| Coffee (3–5 cups/day) | All-cause mortality | RR=0.87 | Meta-analyses |
| Sugar-sweetened beverages (per serving/day) | Type 2 diabetes | RR=1.27 | Meta-analyses |
| Vitamin D supplementation | Mortality, CVD, cancer | ~Null | VITAL trial; Bolland meta |
| Multivitamins | All-cause mortality | RR=0.98 (null) | 21-RCT meta |
| Fish oil (EPA+DHA) | MACE | HR=0.99 (STRENGTH) vs. 0.75 (REDUCE-IT) | Conflicting RCTs |
| Salt reduction (6 g/day) | SBP in hypertensives | −7.1 mmHg | He & MacGregor |
| Exercise (vs. sedentary, meeting guidelines) | All-cause mortality | HR≈0.80 | Multiple large cohorts |
| PM2.5 (per 10 μg/m³) | All-cause mortality | RR=1.08 | WHO; Chen & Hoek [@chen2020] |
:::

::: {.callout .teal}
**Mendelian randomization has overturned many observational findings.** The alcohol "J-curve" (observational RR = 0.75--0.85 for moderate drinking) was undone by MR studies showing monotonically increasing harm (OR = 1.27 per SD increase). Vitamin D's extensive observational protective associations are mostly null in MR. HDL cholesterol's inverse association with CVD proved non-causal. Where MR confirms causality --- LDL → CHD, BMI → diabetes, smoking → cancer --- concordance with RCTs strengthens confidence that the effect is genuinely causal.
:::

### A.9   Genetics {#a-gen}

The central tension in genetics is between large aggregate heritability and minuscule per-variant effects. Polderman et al. [@polderman2015] meta-analyzed 17,804 traits across 2,748 twin publications and 14.6 million twin pairs, finding overall heritability of 49%. Yet individual genetic variants each explain less than 0.1% of variance for complex traits.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 9. A.9 Genetics**

| Trait | Twin h ² | PGS R ² | Per-SNP contribution |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Height | 80–90% | ~25–40% | ~0.003% (12,111 SNPs in Yengo 2022) |
| Intelligence | 50–80% | ~10% | <0.01% |
| BMI | 40–70% | ~8–9% | <0.01% |
| Schizophrenia | 60–80% | ~7–18% liability | <0.05% |
| Major depression | 30–40% | ~2–3% | <0.01% |
| Educational attainment | 40–60% | ~10–15% | <0.01% |
:::

**The candidate gene era was a catastrophe.** Border et al. [@border2019] definitively showed that the 18 most-studied candidate genes for depression --- including 5-HTTLPR --- are all null in adequately powered samples (N \> 100,000). As a set, depression candidate genes were no more associated with depression than randomly selected non-candidate genes. Duncan and Keller [@duncan2011] found that 96% of novel candidate gene-by-environment interaction studies reported significant results, but only 27% of replication attempts did --- consistent with the interpretation that most or all positive cG×E findings are type I errors.

### A.10   Neuroscience {#a-neuro}

Marek et al. [@marek2022], analyzing \~50,000 individuals across ABCD, HCP, and UK Biobank, found that the median reproducible brain-behavior correlation is *r* = 0.01, with the top 1% of effects at *r* \> 0.06. Published small-sample neuroimaging studies (median N ≈ 25) routinely reported correlations of *r* = 0.20 to 0.80 --- inflated by one to two orders of magnitude. Even at N = 1,000, half of statistically significant associations were inflated by at least 100%. Approximately N = 9,500 individuals are needed for 80% power to detect the largest 1% of brain-behavior effects.

Button et al. [@button2013] found the median statistical power of neuroscience studies to be about 21%. Szucs and Ioannidis [@szucs2017] examined 26,841 statistical records from 3,801 neuroscience and psychology papers and found median power of 12%, with significant results reporting median *d* = 0.93 while non-significant results sat at *d* = 0.24 --- the fingerprint of massive selective inflation.

### A.11   Economics & behavioral policy {#a-econ}

Ioannidis, Stanley, and Doucouliagos [@ioannidis2017] analyzed 64,076 estimates from 6,700+ empirical studies across 159 economics research literatures. They found median statistical power of 18%, with roughly 80% of reported effects exaggerated; the typical inflation factor was 2×, and one-third of effects were inflated by a factor of four or more. Doucouliagos [@doucouliagos2011] examined 22,000 partial correlations and found a median absolute partial *r* of 0.173.

The clearest demonstration of publication-to-reality shrinkage comes from DellaVigna and Linos [@dellavigna2022], who compared 26 academic nudge studies to 126 RCTs (covering 23 million individuals) run by the U.S. Office of Evaluation Sciences and the Behavioural Insights Team North America. Academic papers reported an average impact of 8.7 percentage points (a 33% increase over control groups). Government nudge units found 1.4 percentage points (an 8% increase) --- a 6× gap, roughly 70% of which is attributable to selective publication.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 10. A.11 Economics & behavioral policy**

| Finding | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Auto-enrollment 401(k) participation | 37% → 86% | Madrian & Shea [@madrian2001] |
| Opt-out vs. opt-in organ donation | ~99% vs. low teens | Johnson & Goldstein [@johnson2003] |
| Default effects (meta-analytic) | d =0.68, I²=98% | Jachimowicz et al. [@jachimowicz2019] |
| Loss aversion coefficient λ (original) | ~2.25 | Kahneman & Tversky |
| Loss aversion λ (meta-analytic) | 1.31 to 1.96 | Brown et al. [@brown2024] ; others |
| Financial literacy interventions on behavior | ~0.1% variance ( r ≈0.03) | Fernandes, Lynch & Netemeyer [@fernandes2014] |
| Financial literacy (updated meta) | ~3× larger than above | Kaiser et al. [@kaiser2022] |
| Minimum wage employment effect (bias-corrected) | Close to zero | Doucouliagos & Stanley [@doucouliagos2009] |
| Active labor market programs, short run | ~Zero | Card, Kluve & Weber [@card2018] (207 studies) |
| Active labor market programs, 2–3 years later | Modestly positive | Card, Kluve & Weber [@card2018] |
:::

Three independently collected economics corpora --- the Brodeur, Cook & Heyes [@brodeur2020] "Methods Matter" file of causal-inference tests in top-25 econ journals (2015--2018), the Gechert--Havránek--Iršová--Kolcunová [@gechert2022] meta-analysis of the capital-labor elasticity σ (121 studies, 1961--2017), and the Havránek [@havranek2015] meta-analysis of the elasticity of intertemporal substitution (41 micro-panel studies, 1981--2012) --- when placed on the same log *N* × log \|*d*\| axes as psychology's statcheck corpus, produce a combined slope of -0.55 (*r* = -0.84, 272 papers, 75% above *p* \< 0.05). This is essentially identical to psychology's −0.45 (64% shrinkage per tenfold *N*) and close to the −0.5 theoretical slope a pure publication filter would produce. The three corpora span completely different research methods (reduced-form causal inference, structural parameter estimation, and household-panel consumption-Euler regression), different time periods (1961--2018), and different sample-size regimes (σ median *N* ≈ 44, Brodeur ≈ 4,800, EIS ≈ 5,700), yet all three produce the same boundary-hugging scatter. Economics is not a cleaner filter regime than psychology; it is the same filter regime. The 272 paper-level points appear as green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n; the three corpora's filter derivations are documented in the data-extraction appendix.

### A.12   Development economics {#a-dev}

Vivalt [@vivalt2020] compiled the AidGrade database of 15,024 estimates from 635 papers spanning 20 intervention types. The average standardized effect was about 0.12 SD. Heterogeneity was extreme --- predicting the result of the next study from existing studies produced errors 99% as large as the effect itself. Even predicting the correct sign of an effect succeeded only 61% of the time. Government-implemented programs showed significantly smaller effects than academic or NGO implementations.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 11. A.12 Development economics**

| Intervention | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Microcredit (6-country RCTs) | Modest, not transformative; ~7% business profit, could be zero | Banerjee et al. [@banerjee2015] ; Meager 2019 |
| Cash transfers (GiveDirectly) | +13% consumption; fiscal multiplier 2.4–2.7 | Egger et al. [@egger2022] |
| Graduation approach | +8% consumption, +15% assets, B/C ratio 379% by year 10 | Banerjee et al. [@banerjee2015] (Science); Bandiera et al. |
| Insecticide-treated bednets | RR=0.83 child mortality; 5.6 lives/1,000/yr | 22-RCT Cochrane |
| Deworming (short-run, Cochrane) | Little effect on most outcomes | Cochrane reviews |
| Deworming (20-year Kenya follow-up) | +14% consumption, +13% earnings, 37% annualized return | Hamory et al. [@hamory2021] |
| Electrification (quasi-experimental) | +40% income over 40 years | Lipscomb et al. |
| Electrification (RCT) | Null | Lee, Miguel & Wolfram |
| International education RCTs | Median d =0.10 on learning; 0.07 on access | Evans & Yuan [@evans2022] |
:::

::: {.callout .coral}
**The electrification gap is instructive.** Quasi-experimental designs yield estimates an order of magnitude larger than the RCT of the same intervention. This can be read in two ways: quasi-experimental studies are contaminated by confounding, or RCTs miss long-run general-equilibrium effects. The deworming long-run follow-up (20 years out) showing large effects when short-run studies showed little suggests that for interventions whose benefits accumulate over decades, short-run RCTs may genuinely understate impact. The lesson is not that RCTs are wrong but that the time horizon of measurement matters.
:::

### A.13   Education {#a-edu}

Kraft [@kraft2020], analyzing 1,942 effect sizes from 747 education RCTs, found median effect 0.10 SD and weighted mean only 0.04 SD on standardized achievement. The 25th percentile was approximately zero (0.01 SD); the 75th was 0.25 SD. Pre-registered Department of Education studies showed a median of 0.03 SD. Kraft proposed recalibrated benchmarks: **small \< 0.05 SD, medium 0.05--0.20 SD, large ≥ 0.20 SD** --- making his "large" equivalent to Cohen's "small."

Lortie-Forgues and Inglis [@lortieforgues2019] analyzed 141 large-scale education RCTs representing over 1.2 million students and found a mean effect of 0.06 SD. Only 23% of effects were statistically significant. The median Bayes factor was 0.56 --- most results were literally uninformative.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 12. A.13 Education**

| Intervention | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Tutoring (pooled) | d =0.29 | Nickow, Oreopoulos & Quan [@nickow2024] |
| Tutoring at scale (realistic) | d =0.14–0.21 | Kraft, Schueler & Falken [@kraft2024] |
| Class-size reduction (Project STAR) | d =0.15–0.25 | Krueger; Finn & Achilles |
| Class size (Hoxby natural variation) | ≈0, precision rules out > 0.03 SD | Hoxby 2000 |
| Perry Preschool, IQ at age 5 | d =0.87 | Original |
| Perry Preschool, IQ by age 14 | ~0 (faded) | Follow-ups |
| Perry Preschool, life outcomes age 40+ | Large (+20pp HS grad, crime reduction) | Heckman et al. |
| Head Start (cognitive, short-run) | d =0.15–0.25, fades within 1–3y | HSIS |
| Teacher value-added (per SD of teacher quality) | 0.10–0.20 SD annual test scores | Rivkin, Hanushek & Kain [@rivkin2005] ; Chetty et al. [@chetty2014] |
| Replace bottom-5% teacher with average teacher | +$250K lifetime earnings per classroom | Chetty et al. [@chetty2014] |
| Hattie “Visible Learning” average | d =0.40 — inflated 4–7× | Kraft 2020 critique |
:::

### A.14   Political science {#a-poli}

Kalla and Broockman [@kalla2018] synthesized 49 field experiments on campaign contact in general elections and concluded that the best estimate of the average persuasive effect on vote choice is zero. The distribution of test statistics followed a normal distribution nearly perfectly, consistent with null effects. This held across mail, phone, canvassing, and advertising. Narrow exceptions exist: when candidates hold unusually unpopular positions, or when contact occurs very far before election day (though this early persuasion decays).

Get-out-the-vote effects are small but real. Door-to-door canvassing increases turnout by about 4.3 percentage points among those contacted (complier average causal effect), but since only 25--30% of targets are actually reached, the intent-to-treat effect is about 1 percentage point. Standard GOTV mail raises turnout by about 0.5 percentage points per piece. Social-pressure mailings are more effective at 2.3--2.85 percentage points. Robocalls and mass email show no statistically significant effects.

### A.15   Criminology {#a-crim}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 13. A.15 Criminology**

| Intervention | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hot-spots policing | d =0.132, no displacement | Braga et al. [@braga2019] (65 studies) |
| Juvenile delinquency interventions | d =0.10–0.15 | Lipsey & Cullen [@lipsey2007] |
| Drug courts (adult) | Recidivism 50% → 38%; OR=1.65 | Mitchell et al. |
| Scared Straight programs | OR=1.68–1.72 (increase offending) | Petrosino et al. [@petrosino2013] |
| Routine vs. researcher-led practice | Routine is ~half the demonstration effect | Multiple |
:::

### A.16   Sociology & discrimination {#a-soc}

Quillian et al. [@quillian2017] meta-analyzed all U.S. hiring audit studies from 1989--2015 and found whites received 36% more callbacks than African Americans, **with no change over 25 years** despite civil rights legislation, anti-discrimination law, and cultural shifts. Anti-Hispanic discrimination showed a modest decline.

Gender differences are mostly small. Hyde [@hyde2005] found 78% of gender effect sizes were *d* \< 0.35 across 124 effects from 46 meta-analyses; math performance differences are *d* = −0.05 (trivial). Del Giudice [@giudice2012] showed that the multivariate Mahalanobis *D* for personality differences reaches 2.71 at the facet level --- small univariate differences combine into substantial aggregate differences.

Values-affirmation writing interventions [@cohen2006] initially appeared to reduce the racial achievement gap by 40%. A well-powered registered replication [@hanselman2016] found no effect, with precision sufficient to reject benefits larger than *d* = 0.10. Effectiveness appears highly context-dependent.

### A.17   Organizational & management {#a-org}

Management quality explains 20--33% of cross-country and within-country productivity variation (Bloom and Van Reenen 2007; World Management Survey). An RCT providing management consulting to Indian textile firms improved productivity by 17% and profitability by about \$325,000 per year [@bloom2013].

Leadership training (Lacerenza et al. [@lacerenza2017]; 335 samples) shows δ = 0.63 for reactions, 0.73 for learning, 0.82 for behavioral transfer, 0.72 for results --- among the largest intervention effects in applied psychology. General organizational training [@arthur2003] shows *d* ≈ 0.60--0.63. These effects may partly reflect measurement sensitivity to immediate post-training assessment.

Employee selection validity was substantially revised downward by Sackett et al. [@sackett2022]. Corrected validity for cognitive ability dropped from *r* = 0.51 (Schmidt & Hunter [@schmidt1998]) to 0.31 --- a 39% reduction. Structured interviews emerged as the best predictor at 0.42, dethroning cognitive ability from its 25-year position.

### A.18   Marketing & advertising {#a-mkt}

Lewis and Rao [@lewis2015] showed that the *R*² of even a highly profitable ad campaign on individual-level sales is approximately 0.000005 --- essentially zero. The standard deviation of individual-level sales is typically 10 times the mean. Experiments require over 10 million person-weeks to distinguish a profitable campaign from a null effect.

Blake, Nosko and Tadelis [@blake2015] demonstrated this experimentally at eBay: when the company stopped bidding on branded search terms, virtually all traffic arrived organically. The causal effect of brand advertising was near zero. Sethuraman, Tellis and Briesch [@sethuraman2011] found mean short-term advertising elasticity of 0.12 (a 1% ad-spending increase yields 0.12% sales increase), with roughly half of campaigns showing no statistically significant effect.

### A.19   Environmental & climate {#a-env}

**Carbon pricing** reduces emissions by 5--21% across schemes, shrinking to 4--15% after publication-bias correction (Döbbeling-Hildebrandt et al. [@d2024] (2024), Nature Communications). Sweden's carbon tax reduced transport emissions by 11% [@andersson2019].

**Energy conservation nudges** (Allcott 2011 OPOWER): social-norm messaging reduces household electricity use by about 2.0% in the U.S. (range 1.4--3.3%), but only 0.7% in Germany. Pro-environmental behavior interventions produce very small effects: *d* = −0.09 for household climate action [@nisa2019]. After publication-bias correction, climate mitigation nudge effects may approach zero.

**WASH interventions** experienced a dramatic downgrade. Three large RCTs (WASH Benefits in Bangladesh and Kenya; SHINE in Zimbabwe) found no effect on child linear growth and mixed effects on diarrhea --- overturning historical meta-analytic estimates of 23--37% diarrhea reduction. **Clean cookstoves** similarly showed null health effects in the HAPIN trial despite achieving 66% reductions in PM2.5 exposure (birth weight +19.5g, non-significant; no pneumonia reduction).

### A.20   Sports science {#a-sport}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 14. A.20 Sports science**

| Intervention | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Caffeine on endurance performance | d =0.33 (I²=0%) | Meta-analyses |
| Beta-alanine (all uses) | ES=0.18 | Meta-analyses |
| Beta-alanine (0.5–10 min tasks) | ES=0.50 | Meta-analyses |
| Strength training on injury prevention | RR≈0.32 (68% reduction) | Lauersen et al. [@lauersen2014] |
| Stretching alone on injury prevention | Null | Multiple meta-analyses |
| Cold-water immersion on soreness | SMD=−0.37 to −0.89 | Meta-analyses |
| Compression garments on recovery | g =0.40–0.49 | Meta-analyses |
:::

### A.21   Agriculture {#a-ag}

Nitrogen fertilization increases crop yield by about 30--75% relative to no fertilization across global meta-analyses. Enhanced-efficiency fertilizers add another 8--10% on top of standard fertilizer. These effects are 5--50× larger than typical social-science intervention effects measured in comparable terms.

### A.22   COVID-19 {#a-covid}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 15. A.22 COVID-19**

| Intervention | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| mRNA vaccines (phase 3, short-term) | 94–95% RRR; ARR<1.2% | Pfizer; Moderna trials |
| Dexamethasone (ventilated patients) | 35% mortality reduction; NNT=8 | RECOVERY trial |
| Medical/surgical masks | RR=0.95 (NS) | Cochrane [@jefferson2023] |
| Masks in Bangladesh NORM trial | 9.5–11.5% reduction in seroprevalence | Abaluck et al. |
:::

::: {#a-patterns .part-label}
Part II --- Cross-cutting patterns
:::

### A.23   Publication bias inflation factors by literature

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 16. A.23 Publication bias inflation factors by literature**

| Literature | Published | Bias-corrected | Inflation |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| All Cochrane RCTs (median power 13%) | Significant result at p =0.05 | True effect | ~3× |
| Economics (Stanley & Doucouliagos methods) | Reported effect | PET-PEESE corrected | ~2× |
| Social psychology priming / ego depletion | d =0.5–1.0 | d =0–0.10 | 5–∞× |
| Growth mindset interventions | d =0.08 | d =0.02 | ~4× |
| Nudges: academic vs. government scale | 8.7 pp | 1.4 pp | 6× |
| Nutritional epidemiology (single studies vs. MA) | RR=2.2 | RR=0.96 | ~23× |
| Candidate gene×environment | 96% significant (novel) | 27% significant (replications) | ~3.6× |
| NHLBI CV trials, pre- vs. post-registration | 57% positive (pre-2000) | 8% positive (post-2000) | ~7× |
| Antidepressants: FDA vs. published | 94% positive in print | 51% positive in FDA database | ~1.8× positive-rate; ~32% effect-size inflation |
| Small-sample BWAS vs. adequately powered | r =0.20–0.80 | r =0.01–0.06 | 10–80× |
| Preclinical animal stroke studies | Published effect | Corrected effect | ~31% inflation |
| Economics, pre- vs. non-pre-registered (implied) | r =0.36 (schools of fish) | r =0.16 | ~2× |
:::

### A.24   The many-analysts problem

Three landmark crowdsourced reanalyses demonstrate how much effect sizes and significance depend on analytical choices rather than the data alone.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 17. A.24 The many-analysts problem**

| Study | Design | Finding |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Silberzahn et al. [@silberzahn2018] | 29 teams, same soccer red-card dataset | ORs ranged 0.89–2.93; 69% significant, 31% not |
| Botvinik-Nezer et al. [@botviniknezer2020] | 70 teams, same fMRI dataset | No two identical workflows; map overlap near zero |
| Breznau et al. [@breznau2022] | 73 teams, same immigration data | 1,261 unique models; results strongly negative to strongly positive |
| Orben & Przybylski [@orben2019] | Screens & wellbeing, specification curve | 600M+ valid specifications; median β=−0.04 |
:::

### A.25   Observational → experimental reversals

Anglemyer et al. [@anglemyer2014] (2014 Cochrane review) found that on average, observational studies and RCTs produce similar estimates (ratio of ratios 1.04, 95% CI 0.89--1.21). But this average masks consequential discordances: observational associations routinely turn out to be confounded. Mendelian randomization studies have been particularly useful reality checks.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 18. A.25 Observational → experimental reversals**

| Claim | Observational | Experimental / MR |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Moderate alcohol lowers mortality (J-curve) | RR=0.75–0.85 | Monotonic harm; OR=1.27 per SD |
| Vitamin D supplementation reduces mortality | Protective | ~Null (VITAL, Bolland; MR) |
| HDL cholesterol protects against CVD | Strong inverse | Non-causal (MR) |
| β-carotene, vitamin E, selenium | Protective against cancer | Null or harmful in RCTs |
