| HRT protects against CVD | Protective | Harmful (WHI) |
| Rural electrification on income | +40% over 40 years | Null in RCT |
| LDL → CHD, BMI → T2D, smoking → cancer | Causal | Confirmed by MR/RCTs |
:::

### A.26   WEIRD samples and generalization

Henrich, Heine, and Norenzayan [@henrich2010] documented that 96% of psychology research participants come from Western, Educated, Industrialized, Rich, Democratic societies representing 12% of the world's population. WEIRD subjects are frequent outliers --- Ultimatum Game offers (Western \~40% vs. 26% in some small-scale societies), susceptibility to visual illusions, self-concept independence. Effect sizes calibrated on WEIRD samples may not generalize globally.

In development economics, Vivalt [@vivalt2020] found that geography has surprisingly little explanatory power for effect-size variation. A result from Kenya does not even generalize to Kenya very well --- prediction errors are about 99% of actual effect sizes. Whatever drives heterogeneity in treatment effects operates at a finer grain than country or region.

### A.27   The time trajectory of published effects

The decline effect --- the phenomenon that published effects shrink over time as a field matures --- is largely an artifact of reduced publication bias as replication becomes possible. Three data points:

-   NHLBI cardiovascular trials: 57% significant pre-ClinicalTrials.gov (2000), 8% significant afterward (Kaplan & Irvin [@kaplan2015]).
-   Dumas-Mallet et al.: initial highly-cited findings in biomedical research are subsequently refuted or substantially revised about 40% of the time.
-   Fanelli [@fanelli2010]: positive-result rates increased 22% from 1990 to 2007 across disciplines --- not because science got better, but because publication pressures intensified.

## When the world gives you large effects

Appendix A argued that the modal real effect is small. This appendix catalogs the exceptions --- interventions that produce genuinely large effects, survive replication, and translate to field implementation without collapsing. The catalog is not long relative to the appendix that precedes it, and that is the point. Across the full range of things humans have tried to change about human beings, animals, ecosystems, and economies, the interventions that produce order-of-magnitude effects on outcomes we care about number in the dozens, not the thousands. Examining them closely reveals why: they share a specific structural signature, and most candidate interventions do not meet it.

The working definitions: an effect is "genuinely large" if (a) its magnitude sits at or above the 90th percentile of its field's published effect-size distribution, (b) the point estimate is stable under high-quality replication or large-scale implementation, and (c) the bias-corrected estimate remains substantial after accounting for publication selection, small-sample inflation, and the winner's curse. Most of the entries below clear those bars by wide margins. Several are so large that the normal machinery of effect-size statistics gives up --- a *d* of 3 or a number-needed-to-treat of 2 is not a number, it is a therapeutic miracle that predates the tools invented to measure lesser interventions.

### B.1   Infectious disease --- antibiotics, vaccines, antivirals {#b-infectious}

The first and still the largest class of genuinely large effects is the control of infectious disease. An effective antimicrobial against a susceptible pathogen produces cure rates that approach the natural history of the untreated condition in its severity, with NNTs of 2 or 3 to prevent mortality in conditions that were otherwise reliably fatal.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 19. B.1 Infectious disease — antibiotics, vaccines, antivirals**

| Intervention | Condition | Effect | Source / note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Penicillin | Bacterial pneumonia, endocarditis | Mortality ~90% → ~10% | Historical pre/post 1940s |
| Streptomycin | Tuberculous meningitis | Mortality 100% → ~30% | MRC 1948, first modern RCT |
| Smallpox vaccine | Smallpox | ~100% protection; disease eradicated 1980 | WHO |
| Polio vaccine | Paralytic polio | US incidence 58,000/yr → ~0 | Salk/Sabin era |
| Measles vaccine | Measles | 97% effective (2 doses); 60%+ global mortality reduction since 2000 | WHO/CDC |
| HPV vaccine | Cervical cancer (precursors) | ~90% reduction in CIN2+ in vaccinated cohorts | Swedish, UK register studies |
| mRNA COVID vaccines (phase 3) | Symptomatic COVID-19 | 94–95% RRR; VE against severe disease > 90% early | Pfizer, Moderna pivotal trials |
| HAART (combination ART) | HIV / AIDS | Life expectancy near-normal; mortality down >80% | Post-1996 |
| Direct-acting antivirals | Hepatitis C | SVR >95%; a chronic disease now curable | Sofosbuvir era |
| Oseltamivir (early, high-risk) | Influenza hospitalization/mortality | ~25% mortality reduction in hospitalized | IPD meta-analyses |
| Insecticide-treated bednets | Child malaria mortality | RR=0.83 overall; RR=0.45 vs. no nets | Cochrane |
| Artemisinin combination therapy | Uncomplicated falciparum malaria | Cure rate > 95% vs. ~50–70% on older regimens | WHO |
| Oral rehydration therapy | Cholera and diarrheal mortality | Case fatality from 30–50% to < 1% | Mahalanabis, Bangladesh |
:::

The pattern across these interventions is consistent. A single dominant cause (the pathogen) produces a clinical outcome (infection, death) that is essentially absent without that cause. Remove the pathogen and the outcome largely disappears. The effect size is enormous because the counterfactual is catastrophic, the causal chain has one link, and the measurement is unambiguous. This architecture is not available in most of medicine, let alone most of the interventions evaluated in behavioral and social science.

### B.2   Deficiency replacement --- supplying what is missing {#b-replacement}

A second class of large effects comes from supplying a substance the body requires and is not producing in adequate quantity. These interventions do not act on a distant mediator or nudge a multiply-determined outcome; they restore a necessary input, and the outcome follows almost mechanically.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 20. B.2 Deficiency replacement — supplying what is missing**

| Intervention | Condition | Effect | Source / note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Insulin | Type 1 diabetes | Life expectancy < 1 yr → near-normal | Banting/Best 1922 |
| Levothyroxine | Overt hypothyroidism | Symptom normalization; near-complete reversal | Standard of care |
| Vitamin B 12 | Pernicious anemia | Mortality ~90% → near-zero | Post-1926 |
| Vitamin D | Rickets | Disease resolution; near-complete prevention | Well-established |
| Iodine fortification | Goiter, cretinism | Near-elimination in iodine-replete regions | Public health |
| Iron supplementation | Iron-deficiency anemia | Hemoglobin and symptoms normalize | Cochrane |
| Folate fortification | Neural tube defects | ~30–50% reduction | US post-1998 |
| Enzyme replacement (Pompe, Gaucher) | Lysosomal storage diseases | Disease course fundamentally altered | Multiple trials |
| Growth hormone | GH deficiency | Near-normalization of growth | Pediatric endocrinology |
:::

Deficiency replacement is the cleanest case of a dominant causal bottleneck. The deficiency *is* the disease. The treatment removes the deficiency. Whatever else is happening in the organism, the outcome moves because the missing input is restored. Contrast with supplementation in non-deficient populations (vitamin E for cardiovascular disease, multivitamins for mortality, vitamin D for all-cause mortality in replete populations), where effects are null or harmful. The large effect requires the deficiency; without it, the intervention is operating on an outcome the body already has under control.

### B.3   Mechanical and structural interventions {#b-mechanical}

When the dysfunction is mechanical, the intervention can be mechanical, and effects are correspondingly large. These are interventions that restore a physical function --- vision, circulation, airway, joint --- rather than nudging a chemical or behavioral process.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 21. B.3 Mechanical and structural interventions**

| Intervention | Condition | Effect | Source / note |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Cataract surgery | Cataract-related blindness | Near-complete vision restoration; millions/year | Standard procedure |
| Hip replacement | End-stage hip OA | Pain elimination; function restoration; NNT~1 | Canadian and UK registry data |
| Knee replacement | End-stage knee OA | Large pain and function improvement (note: sham effect in mild OA is null) | Cf. Moseley 2002 for milder disease |
| Appendectomy | Appendicitis with perforation | Near-universal survival vs. historical ~100% mortality | Historical |
| Coronary bypass / PCI in STEMI | Acute MI, large ischemic territory | NNT ~30 for mortality; enormous for the subset with cardiogenic shock | Distinct from stable angina (see ORBITA) |
| Pacemakers | Complete heart block, severe bradycardia | Historical mortality dominant → near-normal | Standard |
| Defibrillation (VF/VT) | Shockable cardiac arrest | Survival 0% → 40–70% with early shock | AHA data |
| Tracheostomy / endotracheal intubation | Acute airway obstruction | Near-immediate rescue from certain death | Emergency medicine |
| Dialysis | ESRD | Weeks-to-live → years-to-decades | Standard |
| ECMO (neonatal respiratory failure) | Severe neonatal respiratory failure | The sole Cochrane intervention with very large mortality benefit and high-quality evidence | Pereira et al. [@pereira2012] |
| Cochlear implants | Severe-profound deafness (pre-lingual) | Functional hearing restoration; transformative | Multi-decade evidence |
:::

The structural pattern: the causal pathway is short (intervention acts directly on the physical problem), the counterfactual is catastrophic (death or profound functional loss), and the measurement is unambiguous (the person sees or does not, breathes or does not, lives or does not). Notice the contrast with the arthroscopic knee and stable-angina PCI results in Appendix A: once the mechanical target is not clearly the cause of the symptom, the large effect vanishes. The architecture is doing the work.

### B.4   Cardiovascular and metabolic --- a class of real, modest-to-large effects {#b-cv}

Several pharmacologic interventions in cardiovascular and metabolic disease have effects large enough to be clinically transformative, though the effect sizes are smaller than in the infectious-disease or deficiency-replacement classes. They are included here because the cumulative population impact is enormous and the evidence is as strong as clinical trials produce.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 22. B.4 Cardiovascular and metabolic — a class of real, modest-to-large effects**

| Intervention | Outcome | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Statins, secondary prevention | CV mortality, MI, stroke | Relative risk reduction ~25–30%; NNT 19–21 over 10 years | CTT Collaboration |
| Antihypertensives, per 10 mmHg SBP | Major CV events | RR=0.80; consistent across drug classes | Ettehad et al. [@ettehad2016] |
| SGLT2 inhibitors (empagliflozin) | CV death in T2D with CV disease | HR=0.62 | EMPA-REG OUTCOME 2015 |
| SGLT2 inhibitors (dapagliflozin) | Heart failure hospitalization / CV death | HR=0.74 | DAPA-HF 2019 |
| PCSK9 inhibitors | LDL-C lowering | ~60% additional reduction on top of statin | FOURIER, ODYSSEY |
| Aspirin in acute MI | Vascular mortality | 23% RRR | ISIS-2 1988 |
| Warfarin / DOACs in AF | Stroke prevention | ~65% RRR | AFASAK, multiple pivotal trials |
| Semaglutide 2.4mg (obesity) | Weight loss | ~15% body weight reduction vs. ~2.5% placebo | STEP trials |
| Tirzepatide 15mg (obesity) | Weight loss | ~21% body weight reduction | SURMOUNT-1 |
:::

The GLP-1 and SGLT2 classes deserve particular attention. Most pharmacologic advances in the last two decades have produced effects indistinguishable from prior standards-of-care. These two classes did not --- they produced large enough incremental benefits that the counterfactual-gap argument against large effects is suspended. The reason appears to be genuine biological novelty: both classes act through mechanisms that were not targeted by prior therapies, restoring regulatory pathways that the body was failing to use. They are closer to deficiency replacement than to a typical incremental improvement.

### B.5   Mental health --- the genuine exceptions {#b-mh}

Appendix A documented that most mental-health interventions produce small-to-moderate effects, shrinking substantially when compared against active controls and when measured blind. A small number of interventions break this pattern.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 23. B.5 Mental health — the genuine exceptions**

| Intervention | Condition | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Clozapine | Treatment-resistant schizophrenia | Superior to all other antipsychotics in head-to-head trials; reduces suicide ~25% | InterSePT, Huhn et al. [@huhn2019] |
| Lithium | Bipolar disorder | Reduces suicide ~60–80%; unique among mood stabilizers | Cipriani et al. meta-analyses |
| ECT | Severe treatment-resistant depression | d ≈ 0.9–1.0 vs. sham; response rates 60–80% | UK ECT Review Group 2003 |
| Methylphenidate | ADHD core symptoms (children) | SMD=−0.78 vs. placebo; rapid onset; replicates across settings | Multiple meta-analyses |
| Trauma-focused CBT / EMDR | PTSD | d ≥ 1.0 vs. waitlist; NNT < 4 | Cusack et al. [@cusack2016] |
| CBT for OCD (ERP) | OCD | d ≈ 1.1 vs. waitlist | Multiple meta-analyses |
| Opioid agonist therapy (methadone, buprenorphine) | Opioid use disorder mortality | All-cause mortality rate ratio ~3.2× lower in treatment | Sordo et al. [@sordo2017] |
| Exposure therapy for specific phobia | Specific phobia | Single-session success rates 80–90% | Öst and follow-ups |
:::

The pattern: these interventions work on conditions with a relatively clear target mechanism and they work directly on it. Trauma-focused therapy reprocesses the traumatic memory that *is* the disorder. ERP extinguishes the compulsion that *is* the disorder. Clozapine acts on a dopamine/serotonin profile that the other antipsychotics do not reach. Lithium's anti-suicide effect appears to be a genuine pharmacologic property no other mood stabilizer shares. When a psychiatric intervention has a dominant mechanism and a short causal chain to the outcome, effects are large. When it does not --- most of the SSRI literature, most of mindfulness research, most of psychotherapy against active controls --- effects are small.

### B.6   Education --- the real exceptions {#b-edu}

Appendix A documented that the modal education RCT produces a median effect of *d* = 0.10 and a weighted mean of *d* = 0.04. The exceptions are few, and they are exceptions for structural reasons.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 24. B.6 Education — the real exceptions**

| Intervention | Effect | Structural note |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High-dosage tutoring (pre-K to 12) | ~ d =0.37 overall; larger in early grades | Nickow, Oreopoulos & Quan [@nickow2024] ; short chain, intensive dose, active matching of content to student |
| Teacher value-added (top quartile vs. bottom quartile) | +$250K lifetime earnings per classroom | Chetty, Friedman & Rockoff [@chetty2014] ; small per-year effects compounded over many years of exposure |
| Perry Preschool | Long-run earnings, crime, welfare effects | Heckman; a very high-quality, intensive, early-childhood intervention on an undertreated population |
| Scripted early literacy curricula (developing countries) | d ≈ 0.3–0.5 in some RCTs | Tusome (Kenya), similar; low counterfactual + structured dose |
| Teach at the Right Level (India) | Large gains in basic literacy/numeracy for lagging students | Banerjee et al.; targets the specific skill gap |
| Direct Instruction (Project Follow Through) | Largest effects on basic skills among tested curricula | Relative to other curricula; historical |
| Removing extreme class-size (Tennessee STAR, early grades) | d ≈ 0.2 per ~7-student reduction | Krueger 1999; specific to early grades and large reductions |
:::

The pattern is that "education" is a multiply-determined outcome with small effects per intervention, *except* when the intervention is intensive, targeted at a specific skill gap, delivered against a counterfactual of near-zero instruction, or compounded over many years of exposure. Tutoring satisfies three of those conditions. Teacher value-added compounds over a career. Perry Preschool combined intensity, early timing, and undertreated baseline. The rule is "education RCTs produce small effects"; the exceptions are where the structural conditions for a large effect happen to line up.

### B.7   Policy and behavioral architecture --- changing the default {#b-policy}

The largest behavioral-policy effects in the literature are not from persuasion, education, or incentives. They are from changing the structural default so that the behavior one wants is the path of least resistance.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 25. B.7 Policy and behavioral architecture — changing the default**

| Intervention | Outcome | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Auto-enrollment in 401(k) | Participation | 37% → 86% (Madrian & Shea [@madrian2001] ); persists long-term | Multi-cohort evidence |
| Opt-out organ donation | Registered donors | ~15% (opt-in countries) → 85–99% (opt-out) | Johnson & Goldstein [@johnson2003] |
| EITC expansions | Single-mother labor supply | Large positive employment effects | Eissa & Liebman; Meyer & Rosenbaum |
| Conditional cash transfers (Progresa/Oportunidades) | School attendance, health visits | Large and durable | Schultz; Gertler |
| Large unconditional cash transfers (GiveDirectly) | Consumption, assets | Durable increases in assets and consumption | Haushofer & Shapiro; Egger et al. |
| Graduation programs for ultra-poor | Consumption, assets, mental health | Durable effects at 3 and 7 years across 6 countries | Banerjee et al. [@banerjee2015] Science , Bandiera et al. |
| Smoking bans in public places | Acute coronary events | ~15% reduction in hospital admissions | Multiple pre/post studies, IOM review |
| Graduated licensing | Teen crash mortality | ~20–40% reduction | CDC systematic reviews |
| Seatbelt laws + primary enforcement | Motor vehicle fatalities | Substantial and durable | NHTSA |
| Motorcycle helmet laws | Motorcyclist mortality | ~37% reduction in fatal injury risk | Cochrane |
| Alcohol taxation | Consumption, harm | Elasticity ~−0.5; translates to substantial mortality effects | Wagenaar meta-analyses |
:::

The unifying feature is that these interventions do not attempt to change preferences, knowledge, or motivation. They change the choice architecture so that the default action produces the desired outcome. The person does not have to do anything differently; they have to *not do* something differently. This is why the effects are large where persuasion and education produce small effects on the same outcomes: removing a structural friction is a high-leverage move; arguing someone into better choices is not.

### B.8   Agriculture and environment --- technological leverage {#b-ag}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 26. B.8 Agriculture and environment — technological leverage**

| Intervention | Effect | Note |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Nitrogen fertilizer on grain yield | +30 to +75% | Global meta-analyses; supplying a limiting nutrient |
| Green Revolution semi-dwarf wheat/rice | Regional yields doubled to tripled | 1960s–1980s; architecture change plus fertilizer plus irrigation |
| Irrigation (where water was limiting) | Yields multiplied | Historical and contemporary evidence |
| Bt cotton (where bollworm was dominant pest) | 40–60% yield increases; 50% pesticide reduction | Where conditions match the trait |
| Lead removal from gasoline | Average blood lead fell ~90% in US cohort | Pre/post; enormous population health implications |
| Ozone layer (Montreal Protocol) | Halted and partially reversed ozone depletion | Global; regulatory intervention on a well-defined cause |
| Sulfur dioxide controls (Acid Rain Program) | SO 2 emissions down > 90% in US | Cap-and-trade on a well-defined pollutant |
| Chlorination of drinking water | Waterborne infectious mortality → near-zero in served populations | Early 20th century US cities |
:::

Agricultural and environmental large effects share the architecture with deficiency replacement and infectious-disease control. A limiting factor (nitrogen, water, a specific pest, a specific pollutant) is dominant. The intervention addresses it directly. The outcome responds.

### B.9   Management and firm-level --- information and structure {#b-mgmt}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 27. B.9 Management and firm-level — information and structure**

| Intervention | Outcome | Effect | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Management consulting (SMEs, India) | Productivity, profit | +17% productivity; +$325K/yr profit | Bloom et al. [@bloom2013] |
| Work-from-home (where feasible, pre-COVID) | Performance / satisfaction | Large positive in one large RCT; variable elsewhere | Bloom, Liang, Roberts, Ying 2015 |
| Targeted wage subsidies (hard-to-employ) | Employment | Substantial effects on specific populations | Card, Kluve & Weber [@card2018] |
| Vocational training (long-term follow-up) | Earnings | Small short-term; meaningful long-run | Card et al.; “large” relative to typical short-run nulls |
:::

### B.10   What the exceptions share {#b-pattern}

Examining the preceding sections together yields a checklist. Interventions produce large effects when *most* of the following conditions hold simultaneously:

1.  **A dominant causal bottleneck.** One factor explains most of the variance in the outcome. The pathogen in an infectious disease. The missing hormone in a deficiency. The limiting nutrient in a crop yield. The structural friction in an enrollment decision. Most of behavioral and social science concerns outcomes with no such bottleneck.
2.  **Short causal chain.** The intervention acts directly on or adjacent to the outcome. Antibiotic on the infection. Insulin on glucose regulation. Cataract surgery on the opacity that blocks vision. Chain length is one or two steps, not five.
3.  **Large counterfactual gap.** The comparison is something-versus-nothing, not something-versus-existing-good-treatment. Most of modern medicine is in the second regime, which is why most new drugs produce small effects; the exceptions (HAART, DAAs for HCV, SGLT2s, GLP-1 obesity drugs) opened genuinely new mechanism space and recovered the first regime.
4.  **Well-measured proximal outcome.** Blood pressure moves in days and is measured with high reliability. Vision is present or absent. Mortality is unambiguous. Noisy distal outcomes cap observable effect sizes regardless of intervention potency.
5.  **Compounded or prevented-catastrophe endpoints.** Small per-unit effects can produce enormous lifetime or population effects when applied across many exposures (teacher value-added, statins, seatbelts). And effects on prevention of rare catastrophic events (cardiac arrest rescue, anaphylaxis, suicide) can look mathematically unbounded because the counterfactual outcome is death.
6.  **Rigorous methodology in a pre-specified design.** The interventions above were not identified by scanning for *p* \< 0.05 in small samples. They were tested in large trials against appropriate controls, and the large effects were what remained. Many interventions that pass (1)--(5) in theory fail to produce large effects in practice; large effects without large trials are not credible, because the filter is too strong.

Interventions that satisfy fewer of these conditions produce smaller effects, and interventions that satisfy none of them are the domain of Appendix C. The distribution of "what works, and how much" across science is not random; it is a function of how many of these structural conditions the intervention meets. The small-effects world is not pessimism about human agency. It is the empirical shadow of a structural fact --- that most outcomes humans care about are multiply caused, distally measured, and already embedded in a context of existing partial solutions --- combined with a filtering process that makes the literature pretend otherwise.

## The hellscape catalog --- effects the literature claimed

*This appendix catalogs effects at the magnitudes their original studies reported, with no bias correction, no replication attempt, no meta-analytic shrinkage. Each entry is presented as if the originally published estimate were the true effect. The third column states what the effect would imply for everyday life if it were real. The fourth column gives the published figure. What has happened to these figures under subsequent scrutiny is documented in Appendix A.*

The organizing principle is that the published literature in several fields described a world in which human behavior, cognition, health, and judgment were routinely moved by large fractions of a standard deviation through almost any incidental intervention the researcher could imagine. Taking that literature at face value for the span of this appendix reveals what kind of world would have to exist for the studies to have been describing something real. We do not live in that world. The fact that we do not is Appendix A's core claim restated as a phenomenology.

::: {.callout .coral}
**Reading protocol.** Every effect size in this appendix comes from at least one published, peer-reviewed study --- typically a prominent one, often a widely cited one, in several cases a textbook result. The deadpan presentation is not an endorsement. It is an exercise in what the literature committed us to believing, before replication forced a retreat. Read for tone; the numbers are real.
:::

### C.1   Social priming --- your environment reshapes you continuously {#c-priming}

If the social priming literature of the 1996--2012 era were correct, incidental features of your environment move cognition, judgment, and behavior at effect sizes normally reserved for medication. The following is a sampling.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 28. C.1 Social priming — your environment reshapes you continuously**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Elderly-trait priming slows walking | d =0.82–1.08 | Merely reading words like “Florida” or “bingo” makes you physically slower for the next ~30 seconds | Bargh, Chen & Burrows [@bargh1996] |
| Warm cup → warm personality judgment | r =0.28–0.31 | Briefly holding a hot vs. cold beverage substantially changes how trustworthy you find the next stranger you meet | Williams & Bargh [@williams2008] |
| Money priming → selfish behavior | d =0.34–0.75 | Seeing a picture of cash makes you measurably less helpful for the rest of the session | Vohs, Mead & Goode [@vohs2006] |
| Professor priming → better trivia performance | 13% accuracy gain | Thinking about a professor before a quiz makes you score as if you had studied more | Dijksterhuis & van Knippenberg 1998 |
| Macbeth effect (moral cleansing) | d =0.6–0.9 | Recalling an unethical act makes you prefer hand sanitizer by a large margin | Zhong & Liljenquist [@zhong2006] |
| Cleanliness → moral judgment | d large | Being in a clean room makes you judge moral transgressions more harshly | Schnall, Haidt, Clore & Jordan [@schnall2008] |
| Flag exposure → political shift | d reported large | Glimpsing a US flag shifts political attitudes rightward for 8+ months | Carter, Ferguson & Hassin [@carter2011] |
| Heavy clipboard → weighty judgment | substantial | Holding a heavier clipboard makes you rate a CV as more important | Ackerman, Nocera & Bargh [@ackerman2010] |
| Disgust primes → moral condemnation | d large | A bad smell in the room makes you condemn unrelated moral transgressions more harshly | Schnall, Haidt, Clore & Jordan [@schnall2008] |
| Hand washing → reduces cognitive dissonance | substantial | Washing your hands after a choice makes you less likely to rationalize it | Lee & Schwarz [@lee2010] |
| Fast-food cues → impatience | substantial | Subliminally seeing a fast-food logo makes you less willing to save money | Zhong & DeVoe [@zhong2010] |
| Red → attractiveness judgments | d moderate-large | Wearing red (or having your photo on a red background) substantially raises your perceived attractiveness | Elliot & Niesta [@elliot2008] |
:::

If this literature were correct, a typical day exposes you to dozens of cues each of which moves some aspect of your cognition by a substantial fraction of a standard deviation. Personality is an emergent average over a violent sea of momentary influences. Reliable self-knowledge is impossible because the self is being re-primed every few minutes.

### C.2   Self-regulation and willpower {#c-depletion}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 29. C.2 Self-regulation and willpower**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Ego depletion | d =0.62 (meta-analytic) | Every act of self-control drains a finite budget; restraint in the morning predicts indulgence in the afternoon | Hagger et al. [@hagger2010] meta-analysis |
| Glucose restores self-control | substantial | A lemonade rescues willpower; low blood sugar is moral weakness | Gailliot, Baumeister et al. [@baumeister2007] |
| Decision fatigue → judicial sentencing | large swings by time of day | Parole decisions depend heavily on whether the judge has eaten recently | Danziger, Levav & Avnaim-Pesso [@danziger2011] |
| Power posing (hormonal) | T +20%, C −25% | Two minutes of expansive posture substantially alters your endocrine profile | Carney, Cuddy & Yap [@carney2010] |
| Smiling pen → cartoon enjoyment | 0.82 units (original) | The muscles of your face alone substantially determine whether something is funny | Strack, Martin & Stepper [@strack1988] |
:::

If these findings were real, the self is a limited battery that discharges throughout the day and recharges on sugar; your posture sets your hormones; your zygomaticus sets your emotions. Moral evaluation of another person should probably condition on their most recent blood glucose reading and the chair they are sitting in.

### C.3   Stereotype effects and implicit attitudes {#c-stereo}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 30. C.3 Stereotype effects and implicit attitudes**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Stereotype threat (standardized-test performance) | d > 0.5 in many published studies | Mentioning demographics before a test substantially alters group-average performance; much of the Black–White test-score gap is a priming effect | Steele & Aronson [@steele1995] and a large derivative literature |
| IAT predicts discriminatory behavior | original r =0.274 | A 5-minute reaction-time task measures unconscious attitudes that cause real-world discrimination | Greenwald, McGhee & Schwartz [@greenwald1998] and derivatives |
| Implicit bias training changes behavior | substantial short-run | Brief training meaningfully reduces discriminatory behavior in hiring, policing, medicine | Widely assumed; largely unreplicated |
| Math-prime stereotype vulnerability (Asian women) | substantial | Priming either “Asian” or “woman” before a math test substantially shifts performance | Shih, Pittinsky & Ambady [@shih1999] |
| Soccer-referee red cards and skin tone | OR ≈ 1.4 in typical specification | Professional referees issue disciplinary cards at meaningfully different rates based on player skin tone | Silberzahn et al. [@silberzahn2018] original data; depended heavily on analytic choices |
:::

If these findings were correct at the magnitudes claimed, most standardized testing is a priming experiment and the gender gap in STEM is a half-hour intervention away from resolution. A brief unconscious-bias module would measurably change hiring outcomes. The fact that none of the above has translated to a demonstrable real-world intervention, despite massive investment, is itself data.

### C.4   Candidate-gene studies --- a single polymorphism determines your fate {#c-genes}

Between roughly 1998 and 2015, a large literature claimed that individual genetic variants --- 5-HTTLPR in the serotonin transporter, DRD4 in the dopamine D4 receptor, COMT, MAOA --- had substantial main effects or gene-by-environment interactions on psychological and behavioral outcomes. Published results were significant in 96% of novel reports (Duncan & Keller [@duncan2011]). Replication rates in independent samples were 27%. The full set of claims, taken at published face value, implied the following.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 31. C.4 Candidate-gene studies — a single polymorphism determines your fate**

| Claim (at published magnitude) | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| 5-HTTLPR short allele + life stress → depression | Whether you develop depression after a bad year is substantially determined by which variant of a single gene you carry | Caspi et al. [@caspi2003] |
| MAOA low-activity + maltreatment → antisocial behavior | A single polymorphism substantially moderates whether childhood abuse produces a violent adult (“warrior gene”) | Caspi et al. [@caspi2002] |
| DRD4 7-repeat → novelty seeking, ADHD, parenting | A single dopamine-receptor variant substantially predicts personality and clinical outcomes across domains | Benjamin et al. [@benjamin1996] and many derivatives |
| COMT val/met → cognition, pain, psychosis | A single amino-acid substitution substantially predicts cognitive performance, pain sensitivity, and psychosis risk | Extensive 2000s literature |
| OXTR polymorphisms → empathy, trust, parenting | Variants in the oxytocin-receptor gene substantially predict social-cognitive outcomes | Numerous 2005–2015 |
:::

If this literature had been correct, behavioral traits of interest would be substantially explained by single variants, and a small commercial genotyping panel could tell you your likely depression risk, your violence risk, your parenting style, your cognitive profile, and your empathy trait. The entire candidate-gene paradigm collapsed when adequately-powered GWAS (typically requiring N \> 10,000) showed that the real genetic architecture of these traits is polygenic with thousands of variants each explaining \< 0.1% of variance [@border2019].

### C.5   Brain imaging --- neural correlates at implausible magnitudes {#c-brain}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 32. C.5 Brain imaging — neural correlates at implausible magnitudes**

| Finding class | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Brain-wide associations with behavior in small-sample fMRI (2000s–2010s) | r = 0.5–0.8 | Specific brain regions substantially explain individual differences in personality, politics, decision-making; a quick scan reveals deep aspects of character | Numerous N < 40 studies |
| “Voodoo correlations” in social neuroscience | r > 0.8 with personality measures | Self-report measures of love, empathy, rejection map onto specific brain regions at near-ceiling correlations | Studies critiqued by Vul, Harris, Winkielman & Pashler [@vul2009] |
| Neuroeconomic predictors of real-world choice | substantial | fMRI signals in the reward circuit predict commercial, political, and financial behavior | Early 2000s neuromarketing |
| Libet-style “no free will” effect | strong | Your brain commits to your actions hundreds of milliseconds before you are aware of deciding | Libet 1983 and extensions |
| Dead salmon shows emotional responses (control critique) | significant voxels | Without multiple-comparison correction, a dead fish exhibits emotion-processing activation | Bennett et al. [@bennett2009] — cited here as the critique that defined the problem |
:::

If the small-sample BWAS literature had been correct, a 30-minute scan could substantially profile a person's personality, political attitudes, and life outcomes. Marek et al. [@marek2022] established that the true correlations at adequate sample sizes are in the *r* = 0.01 range --- the published literature was overstating by one to two orders of magnitude.

### C.6   Education and self-improvement at original billing {#c-edu}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 33. C.6 Education and self-improvement at original billing**

| Finding | Published framing / effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 10,000 hours → expert performance | Deliberate practice as the dominant predictor | Expertise is almost entirely a function of practice; innate differences are small | Ericsson, Krampe & Tesch-Römer 1993; popularized by Gladwell |
| Grit predicts academic and life outcomes beyond conscientiousness | r > 0.3 unique variance in early papers | A brief scale measures a trait that substantially predicts who succeeds, independent of the Big Five | Duckworth et al. [@duckworth2007] |
| Growth mindset interventions | d = 0.3–0.5 in early studies | A brief classroom intervention substantially raises achievement, especially among disadvantaged students | Dweck, Blackwell et al. |
| Brain training → fluid intelligence | d large in early reports | N-back and similar training substantially raises IQ, with far-transfer to academic and professional outcomes | Jaeggi et al. [@jaeggi2008] |
| Learning styles matching | assumed large | Tailoring instruction to a student’s “visual” vs. “auditory” style substantially improves learning | Wide belief, vanishing evidence |
| Mozart effect | d large short-term | Listening to classical music briefly substantially raises spatial reasoning; early-childhood exposure permanently raises IQ | Rauscher, Shaw & Ky [@rauscher1993] ; stronger claims in popularization |
| Nun Study — early-life writing predicts Alzheimer’s | very strong | Autobiographical complexity at age 22 substantially predicts dementia risk 60 years later | Snowdon et al. |
:::

If this literature had been correct, closing achievement gaps would require only a brief psychological intervention, expertise would be a function of practice time alone, and IQ would be trainable. The actual state of evidence after bias correction is in Appendix A: growth mindset produces *d* = 0.02 in the highest-quality studies; brain training has zero far transfer; learning-styles matching has no reliable empirical support; deliberate practice explains 4% of variance in education and 1% among elite performers.

### C.7   Nutritional and lifestyle epidemiology {#c-nutri}

Schoenfeld and Ioannidis [@schoenfeld2013] examined 50 common cookbook ingredients and found that 40 of the 50 had been associated with cancer risk in published studies, with an average published relative risk of 2.20 across the set. Meta-analyses collapsed these associations to RR ≈ 0.96 --- a 23-fold inflation factor from single studies. Taken at published face value, the last forty years of nutritional epidemiology committed the reader to something like the following.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 34. C.7 Nutritional and lifestyle epidemiology**

| Claim (at original published magnitude) | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Coffee → cancer (varied specific sites) | Multiple daily exposures substantially change your oncologic risk | Pre-2000s epidemiology; since largely reversed |
| Eggs → cardiovascular death | Daily eggs substantially raise CVD mortality | Pre-RCT era; reversed |
| Moderate alcohol protective for CV mortality (J-curve) | One to two drinks daily substantially lowers all-cause mortality vs. abstention | Decades of observational; contradicted by MR [@holmes2014] |
| Vitamin E, β-carotene, selenium → cancer prevention | Inexpensive supplements substantially reduce cancer and CV mortality | Observational; null/harmful in RCTs (SELECT, CARET, ATBC) |
| HDL cholesterol protects against CVD | Pharmacologic HDL elevation should substantially reduce CV events | MR and RCTs (torcetrapib etc.) show non-causal |
| HRT protects against CVD | Long-term hormone therapy meaningfully reduces heart attacks | WHI [@investigators2002] reversed this |
| Red meat → cancer | Normal Western consumption substantially raises cancer risk | Associations much weaker after bias correction |
| Low-fat diet → CV protection | A population-level shift in macronutrients substantially reduces CVD | Women’s Health Initiative dietary trial: null |
:::

If the observational nutrition literature had been correct, diet would be the dominant lever for population health, supplements would have transformed longevity by 1990, and hormone replacement would have halved cardiovascular disease in postmenopausal women. Randomized trials have systematically reversed the largest of these claims.

### C.8   Economics, nudges, and campaign persuasion {#c-econ}

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 35. C.8 Economics, nudges, and campaign persuasion**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Academic nudge studies (social comparison, defaults, framing) | 8.7 percentage points average | Low-cost behavioral interventions produce substantial real-world changes when deployed at population scale | DellaVigna & Linos [@dellavigna2022] — academic sample |
| Microcredit as poverty reduction | Substantial positive effects | Small loans are a general-purpose anti-poverty instrument at population scale | 1990s observational and small-sample reports |
| Door-to-door canvassing on vote choice | Several pp per contact in early papers | A small number of well-targeted doorstep conversations could decide a national election | Pre-Kalla-Broockman literature |
| Contact hypothesis on prejudice | d large in early work | Brief positive cross-group contact substantially reduces prejudice long-term | Paluck & Green lit reviews reveal more mixed evidence |
| LaCour canvassing on gay marriage attitudes | d very large, durable | A single 20-minute conversation permanently shifts political attitudes on polarized issues | LaCour & Green [@lacour2014] — retracted 2015 for fabricated data |
| Financial literacy education → behavior | substantial in early work | Classroom financial education substantially improves real-world financial decisions | Fernandes, Lynch & Netemeyer [@fernandes2014] collapse this to r ≈ 0.04 |
| CEO effects on firm performance | reported large | Hiring the right CEO substantially determines shareholder returns | Bertrand & Schoar find small effects; most variance is firm and industry |
| Hurricane-name matching on charitable giving | d reported large | Sharing a first letter with a hurricane’s name substantially raises your probability of donating | Chandler et al. [@chandler2008] |
| Name-letter effect (Dennis → dentist) | substantial | People are meaningfully more likely to choose careers and spouses sharing letters with their own name | Pelham et al. [@pelham2002] series |
:::

If this literature were real, nudges would scale, campaigns would decide elections at small budgets, canvassing would resolve culture-war disputes, and the career you chose was substantially determined by your own first initial. The actual scale effects (1.4 pp for nudges at government scale), the actual canvassing effects on vote choice (indistinguishable from zero in Kalla & Broockman [@kalla2018]), and the actual robustness of the name-letter literature to confounders are documented in Appendix A.

### C.9   Parapsychology --- the limit case {#c-psi}

The strongest test of whether the standard methodological toolkit of psychology can manufacture false positives at publication-quality effect sizes is whether it can produce significant evidence for effects known in advance to be impossible. It can.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 36. C.9 Parapsychology — the limit case**

| Finding | Published effect | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Precognition of future stimuli | 9 experiments, p < 0.05 in 8 | The effect of a future event can be detected in present behavior; time-reversed causation in human cognition | Bem 2011 JPSP |
| Telepathic ganzfeld hit rates | ~32% vs. 25% chance | Mind-to-mind information transfer operates at a small but reliable rate | Honorton and meta-analyses |
| Global Consciousness Project — RNG deviations during major events | p small across events | Collective human attention substantially influences physical random-number generators | PEAR lab and successors |
:::

Bem [@bem2011] was published in the field's leading journal using its standard methods. The study is methodologically indistinguishable from hundreds of uncontested publications. It is included here because it establishes the upper bound on what the standard toolkit can produce: a result with *p* \< 0.05 for an effect that is nomologically impossible. If the methods that produced Bem also produced the rest of the social priming literature, the results are not differentially credible.

### C.10   Animal models and preclinical translation {#c-preclin}

The preclinical animal literature reports substantial therapeutic effects in roughly every disease model in which it is tested. If taken at published face value, thousands of interventions should work in humans. Approximately 5% actually do [@ineichen2024]. Several representative classes:

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 37. C.10 Animal models and preclinical translation**

| Claim (at preclinical published magnitude) | Implication if true | Source |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Hundreds of neuroprotective agents for stroke | Dozens of drugs should prevent stroke damage in humans | ALL failed in human trials until tPA |
| Many anti-amyloid agents for Alzheimer’s | Reducing amyloid plaque in mice should translate to substantial cognitive benefit in humans | Decades of failures before marginal anti-amyloid antibody effects |
| Cancer biology reproducibility | Landmark oncology findings should reliably replicate across labs | Errington et al. [@errington2021] : effects 85% smaller; 40% replication rate |
:::

Publication bias in preclinical stroke inflates effects by \~31% [@sena2010] and small-sample bias compounds this. If the full preclinical literature had been correct, modern medicine would already have cured the major neurodegenerative and cerebrovascular diseases, rather than confronting them.

### C.11   What taking the literature at face value commits you to {#c-sum}

Collecting the appendix into a single inventory of the world implied by the published literature before replication arrived:

-   Every ambient environmental cue --- cup temperature, object weight, color, smell, background image, posture --- substantially reshapes your cognition and judgment within minutes.
-   Self-control is a quantifiable liquid battery that discharges across the day and recharges on sugar, with measurable consequences for judicial, professional, and moral decisions.
-   Standardized testing is largely a priming experiment, and much of the racial and gender achievement gap is manipulable in a half-hour session.
-   Specific individual genetic variants substantially determine depression, aggression, personality, cognition, and parenting.
-   A 30-minute MRI scan reveals deep individual differences at correlations of *r* \> 0.7.
-   Expertise is overwhelmingly practice; talent is small; IQ is trainable; brief classroom interventions close achievement gaps.
-   Diet is the dominant driver of chronic disease; supplements substantially extend life; hormone replacement protects the heart.
-   Behavioral nudges solve policy at population scale; canvassing decides elections; microcredit ends poverty; financial literacy education fixes household finance.
-   The careers you choose and the people you marry are non-trivially determined by letter-match heuristics with your own name.
-   Precognition and telepathy are measurable phenomena at small but reliable effect sizes, and collective consciousness perturbs physical hardware.
-   Almost every preclinical therapeutic finding translates to human benefit.

None of these propositions, taken at the magnitudes the original literature claimed, survived adequate-powered replication. Most survived partially, with effect sizes one-half to one-tenth of the published values and heterogeneity sufficient to prevent confident application. Several are genuine methodological artifacts of a pre-registration, pre-replication era and have vanished entirely under scrutiny.

The point of Appendix C is not that the investigators who produced these findings were uniformly mistaken or dishonest. Most were neither. The point is that the standard methodological toolkit, operating on small samples, with undisclosed analytic flexibility, in the presence of strong publication filters, was capable of producing a literature that described a world that does not exist. That literature was taken seriously for decades. It shaped clinical practice, educational policy, public health messaging, and the popular understanding of human nature. The gap between the world Appendix C describes and the world Appendices A and B describe is the scale of the correction still in progress.

*The upshot of Appendix C: the literature we retired was a literature describing a universe whose rules differ from ours. We do not live in a world where holding a warm cup rewires trust by a third of a standard deviation. We lived for two decades in one that acted like we did.*

## Appendix D --- Replication pairs: original vs. large-N replication

The current replication-pair build contains 1,451 usable original/replication endpoint pairs. Across those rows, 81% of effects shrink and 42% drop below the p \< .05 boundary at the replication sample size.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 38. Appendix D — Replication pairs: original vs. large-N replication**

| # | Study | N orig | d orig | N repl | d repl | Replication |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |

| 3 | Schooler verbal overshadowing (RRR1) | 90 | 0.80 | 2,319 | 0.03 | RRR Alogna 2014 |

| 479 | Raley 2012 (sociology and criminology) | 7,838 | 0.10 | 2,888 | 0.04 | SCORE main |
| 480 | Boehm 2015 (psychology and health) | 4,458 | 0.10 | 4,971 | 0.04 | SCORE main |
| 481 | Boehm 2015 (psychology and health) | 4,458 | 0.10 | 6,126 | 0.01 | SCORE main |
| 482 | Raley 2012 (sociology and criminology) | 2,605 | 0.10 | 392 | 0.03 | SCORE main |
| 483 | Boehm 2015 (psychology and health) | 4,458 | 0.10 | 4,971 | 0.14 | SCORE main |
| 484 | Hiskey 2018 (political science) | 2,236 | 0.10 | 2,042 | 0.10 | SCORE main |

:::

### Aggregate replication-project summaries

Some coordinated replication projects released aggregate meta-analytic statistics as an addition to per-experiment data. These are reported below.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 39. Aggregate replication-project summaries**

| Project | Experiments | Orig d (median) | Repl d (median) | Replication rate | Notes |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Reproducibility Project: Cancer Biology (Errington et al. 2021) | 50 experiments from 23 papers; 158 effects | 2.96 | 0.43 | 46% (39/97 positive effects on ≥3 of 5 criteria) | mean SMD 5.41 → 1.19; 85% effect-size shrinkage; animal experiments 12% replication, in vitro 54% |
| SCORE project (Tyner et al. 2026, Nature) | 274 claims from 164 papers across 6 disciplines; 282 plotted here from the analyst_data.RData master file | 0.49 | 0.25 | 55.1% overall (Tyner 2026); ≈50% shrinkage in median d | OSF repo g5sny/bzfgy; per-claim partial correlations converted to Cohen’s d via standard formula |
| Experimental philosophy replicability (Cova et al. 2021) | 40 replications of published x-phi studies; 33 with complete pairs | — | — | 70% (24/34 with sufficient power) | mean original r ≈ 0.39 → mean replication r ≈ 0.34; per-study data from OSF XPhiReplicability_CompleteData.csv |
:::

### Coverage and known gaps

The pairs plotted above and listed in the main table are the most rigorous direct replications I was able to verify from primary sources. A handful of replication targets were checked but yielded no extractable rows, and are noted here for completeness rather than hidden.

Within RP:CB, seven further original papers were checked directly against the eLife collection and the RP:CB project summary in Errington et al. 2021 *eLife* 10:e67995. Three --- Possemato et al. 2011 *Nature* (PHGDH), Prahallad et al. 2012 *Nature* (BRAF/EGFR feedback), and Metallo et al. 2011 *Nature* (reductive glutamine metabolism) --- were registered for replication but the experiments were never completed; their OSF projects contain no data. Four others --- Bhang et al. 2015 *Nat Med* (ClonTracer), Zhang et al. 2013 *Nature* (CRM1), Morrison et al. 2012 *Science* (mouse OXPHOS), and Schwitalla et al. 2013 *Cell* (NF-κB intestinal tumor initiation) --- were outside RP:CB's 2010--2012 sampling frame or were misidentified in my earlier search and have no RP:CB replication on record. Zero additional pairs were added from these seven. The total RP:CB contribution in the main table (24 pairs from 15 papers) is the complete set that could be verified from the published Replication Studies at the time of writing.

The SCORE master dataset (Tyner et al. 2026 *Nature* 652:143--150), made public through the OSF repositories g5sny and bzfgy, was pulled directly from the `analyst_data.RData` analyst file and joined on claim ID between `orig_outcomes` (825 records) and `repli_outcomes` (427 records, filtered to version-of-record). Of 309 merged version-of-record rows, 282 had non-missing values for all four required fields (original N, original partial correlation, replication N, replication partial correlation) and are included in the main table. Effect sizes were converted from SCORE's native partial-correlation metric to Cohen's *d* using the standard *d* = 2*r*/√(1−*r*²) formula. Five additional SCORE-related rows from Landy et al. 2020 *Psychological Bulletin* and Breznau et al. 2022 *PNAS* are not plotted because the original effect sizes were unpublished pilots or not scalar; they would appear only as replication-side points.

The Protzko, Krosnick, Nelson et al. "High-Replicability Research" project (16 effects × 3 stages = 48 data points from four labs) was retrieved from its OSF materials and initially considered for inclusion as a counter-pattern dataset, since its preregistered discovery--self-replication--adversarial-replication design does not produce the shrinkage pattern that the selection-filtered literature does. The project paper was published in *Nature Human Behaviour* 8:311--319 (2024) but was **retracted in September 2024** at the publisher's initiative, on the grounds that the editors no longer had confidence in the reliability of the findings and conclusions. In keeping with the rule that this note relies only on primary sources in good standing, the 48 Protzko rows are not included in the main table and not plotted. The retraction itself is a useful data point about the literature, but the specific effect-size values cannot be cited here as settled evidence.

The preregistered-hypotheses curve on the empirical density plot is drawn from two sources. The first is Scheel, Schijen & Lakens [@scheel2021], who assembled the complete corpus of 71 psychology Registered Reports published through the end of 2018 and coded whether each study's first preregistered hypothesis was supported. The OSF dataset for that paper (`positive_results_in_registered_reports_data.tsv`) contains only the binary supported/not-supported coding, so the per-hypothesis effect sizes and sample sizes had to be extracted from the individual Registered Reports themselves. Of the 71 articles, 32 reported both an exact *N* and a native-metric effect statistic (*d*, *t*, *F*, *r*, partial η², or χ²) that could be cleanly converted to Cohen's *d* without back-calculation from *df*. The other 39 were excluded because the reported test statistic could not be converted to *d* without introducing estimation error (e.g., unstandardized regression coefficients without SEs, Bayes factors only, multilevel models with unusual *df* structures).

The second source is the Psychological Science Accelerator's Rapid-Response project **PSA-CR001** (Dorison et al. 2022, *Affective Science*) [@dorison2022], a 52-country preregistered test of loss-versus-gain framing in COVID-19 health messaging. The study preregistered four primary confirmatory outcomes (behavioral intentions, two subscales of policy support, and information seeking) and posted country-level effect sizes with 95% CIs on OSF. I pooled each primary outcome across the 52 countries via sample-size-weighted average of \|*d*\|, producing four preregistered-publication rows, each with pooled *N* ≈ 15,772 (the combined sample across all countries after applying the PSA's minimum-per-country *N* ≥ 25 cutoff).

The combined preregistered corpus thus contains 36 rows. Their median \|*d*\| is 0.31 --- between the replicated (0.28) and the original (0.60) distributions, which is what the shrinkage hypothesis predicts. The four PSA-CR001 rows sit near the low end of the combined distribution (\|*d*\| between 0.02 and 0.10 at very large *N*), consistent with what a genuinely preregistered study across 52 countries produces when no file-drawer filter is present.

### Multi-lab coordinated projects: per-lab data availability

Three separate attempts were made to pull per-lab (or per-country, per-site) effect sizes from the major coordinated multi-lab projects so that we could examine within-project heterogeneity independently of the replication-pair corpus. The results of those attempts are documented here so that readers who want to go deeper on lab-to-lab variability can find the extracted data or see what remains unavailable.

**Registered Replication Reports.** The 10 major published RRRs (Alogna verbal overshadowing Study 1, Alogna Study 2, Hagger ego depletion, Wagenmakers facial feedback, Cheung commitment/forgiveness, Eerland grammatical aspect, McCarthy hostile priming, O'Donnell professor priming, Bouwmeester intuitive cooperation, Verschuere moral self) together contributed 205 verified per-lab rows after applying each project's primary-analysis inclusion rules (e.g., McCarthy's 22-lab primary analysis, O'Donnell's 23-lab main analysis). Per-lab effect sizes were converted to Cohen's \|*d*\| from each project's posted forest-plot input: 23 rows came with Cohen's *d* directly (Hagger), 53 as risk differences with 95% CIs (Alogna Study 1 and Study 2), and 129 as raw mean differences with 95% CIs (Cheung, Eerland, McCarthy, O'Donnell, Bouwmeester, Verschuere, Wagenmakers). For the non-*d* rows, the conversion used the standard rearrangement *d* = 2·\|effect\|/(SE·√*N*), where SE is derived from the reported CI width as (CI_upper − CI_lower)/(2·1.96). The per-lab dataset (205 rows) is **not plotted on the main density plot**, because the 10 RRRs are each already represented as the replication side of one of the 555 original--replication pairs. Plotting them again as individual lab contributions would double-count the same replication evidence. The 205-row lab-level CSV is available as a downloadable artifact for readers interested in within-study heterogeneity. Of the 226 expected combinations from the announced RRR protocols, 21 labs were excluded for falling under each project's own primary-analysis rules (sample-size thresholds, native-English criteria, MTurk exclusions, and ancillary-only sample memberships), matching the RRRs' published analyses.

**Psychological Science Accelerator.** An attempt to pull per-site confirmatory effect sizes across all published PSA projects returned usable data only for PSA-CR001 (Loss-Gain framing, 208 country×hypothesis cells, of which the 4 pooled rows shown above enter the main density plot). Published PSA projects where per-site files remained inaccessible through the environment's OSF interface included PSA001 (Social Faces), Many Smiles (facial-feedback, Coles et al. 2022), PSA006 (CNI model of moral dilemmas, Bago et al. 2022 --- the OSF node returns a 410 Gone error as of April 2026), and SPAM-L (semantic priming across languages). PSA002, PSA003, PSA005, PSA008, PSA009, and the JTF-funded projects are listed on the PSA website but lack published Stage 2 papers with country-level confirmatory data as of April 2026. The per-country PSA-CR001 extract (208 cells with *N* ≥ 25) is available as a downloadable artifact; the other PSA projects will require direct OSF collaboration with project leads to obtain clean per-site confirmatory files.

**Many Labs 1--5.** An attempt to extract per-lab effect sizes across the five Many Labs projects (target: \~4,000 effect×lab combinations) failed at the data-access layer: the Many Labs OSF repositories store per-lab analyses in child components with nested directories, and the OSF API does not expose them cleanly for bulk retrieval. A handful of replicate-group-level rows could be pulled from one ML2 effect (Trolley Dilemma 2) as a demonstration, but these do not correspond to individual labs and are not suitable for within-project heterogeneity analysis. The replication-pair corpus already contains the ML1--ML5 project-level findings (13 from ML1, 28 from ML2, 10 from ML3, 1 from ML4, 6 from ML5) as their primary contribution to the density plot and the arrow plot. Recovering the \~4,000 lab-level rows would require direct scripting inside the OSF environment or collaboration with the Many Labs project leads.

### Statcheck general-literature corpus (@fig-published-d-vs-n) {#statcheck-general-literature-corpus-figure-2}

The @fig-published-d-vs-n scatter is built from the Nuijten et al. 2016 export on OSF (`150211FullFile_AllStatcheckData_Automatic1Tail.csv` at osf.io/gdr4q/download) as a fallback after the full 688,112-row Hartgerink 2016 DANS dataset could not be retrieved in the extraction environment. The fallback file contains 258,105 raw APA-formatted test results from 30,717 articles across eight psychology journals, 1985--2013: JPSP (44.3% of retained tests), Developmental Psychology, PLOS, Journal of Consulting and Clinical Psychology, JEP: General, Psychological Science, Journal of Applied Psychology, and Frontiers in Psychology.

Filtering retained 163,322 individual tests from 13,842 papers. The filter stages were: drop 24,961 rows where statcheck flagged internal inconsistency (`Error` or `DecisionError`); drop 9,289 rows with both df fields blank (mostly *Z* rows); drop 46,202 *F* rows with numerator df \> 1 (omnibus tests that cannot be converted to a single *d*); drop 11,110 χ² rows with df ≠ 1 and 2,450 χ²(1) rows without an explicit *N*; drop 591 rows with non-finite converted *d*; drop 178 rows failing the quality filter (*N* \< 4 or *N* \> 10,000,000 or \|*d*\| \> 30). After filtering, the surviving test types are *F*(1, df) at 86,681 rows, two-sample *t* at 60,186 rows, *r* at 9,036 rows, and χ²(1) at 7,419 rows. *N* was recovered from df via *N* = df + 2 for two-sample *t* and *r*, *N* = df2 + 2 for *F*(1, df2), and from the explicit `N =` field for χ²(1). Cohen's *d* was computed test-by-test: *d* = 2*t*/√df for *t*; *d* = 2√(*F*/df2) for *F*(1, df2); *d* = 2*r*/√(1 − *r*²) for *r*; *d* = 2φ/√(1 − φ²) with φ = √(χ²/*N*) for χ²(1).

For the scatter, the 163,322 tests were collapsed to 13,842 paper-level rows by taking the median \|*d*\| and median *N* within each paper. Four additional rows were dropped from the plotted subset (13,838 plotted) because their collapsed median effect size was zero or otherwise invalid for log-scale display. The regression line is a log--log OLS fit across those 13,838 points: slope −0.4465, intercept 0.678, *r*² = 0.42. Translated to the figure's framing: a tenfold increase in *N* corresponds to a 64.2% reduction in reported \|*d*\|.

**Methodological caveat worth flagging.** All *t* rows in the fallback corpus were treated as two-sample by default, because the statcheck package does not distinguish one-sample/paired *t* from two-sample *t*. Psychology's top journals use heavy within-subjects designs (repeated-measures ANOVA, paired *t*-tests), for which the two-sample formula inflates *d* by a factor of roughly √2. A sensitivity check run by the extraction pipeline shows that if every *t* were instead treated as one-sample/paired, median \|*d*\| across the corpus would drop from 0.72 to 0.58. The cross-section pattern (cloud hugging the significance boundary, 87% above *p* \< 0.05, regression slope near −0.45) is robust to this assumption; the specific median is not. Readers who want the raw test-level data or the full filter-accounting log can find `statcheck_tests.csv` (163,322 rows), `statcheck_studies.csv` (13,842 rows), and `statcheck_summary.md` in the project's downloadable artifacts.

### Brodeur economics corpus (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#brodeur-economics-corpus-figure-2-gold-squares}

110 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Brodeur, Cook & Heyes [@brodeur2020] "Methods Matter" replication package for their 2020 *AER* paper on *p*-hacking in causal-inference economics. That paper harvested 21,740 test statistics from 684 articles across the top--25 economics journals, 2015--2018, stratified by causal-inference design (RCT, RDD, DiD, IV).

Of the 21,740 test rows in the cleaned CSV (`methods_matter_clean.csv`), only 1,350 have a non-missing `sample_size` field --- the column was populated inconsistently in the original Stata file and many articles lost the *N* metadata in the conversion. Aggregating those 1,350 tests to article-level medians (analogous to the statcheck processing) yields 112 articles with both median test-level *N* and median derived *d*; two further articles had median \|*d*\| = 0 exactly (all their tests yielded round-to-zero effects) and were dropped for log-axis compatibility. The remaining 110 are green circles.

Effect sizes were derived from the de-rounded *t*-statistics via the standard formula *d* = 2*t*/√df, treating sample_size as df. This is the same conversion used for *t*-rows in the statcheck corpus. For tests reported with coefficients and standard errors instead of *t*, the *t* was recomputed as coef/se before conversion.

The 110-article sample is a *convenience subset* of the full 684--article Brodeur corpus, not a random sample. Articles whose Stata files preserved the `sample_size` column are disproportionately those where the authors reported *N* prominently in their tables, which may correlate with study design (RCTs report *N* clearly; some DiD papers collapse data at aggregated levels that complicate *N* attribution). The stratification within the 110 is: 53 DiD articles (median *d* = 0.055, median *N* = 9,474), 25 RCT articles (*d* = 0.135, *N* = 1,093), 21 IV articles (*d* = 0.123, *N* = 4,441), and 13 RDD articles (*d* = 0.112, *N* = 3,596). Until the full corpus can be recovered with *N* attached, the 110-article overlay should be read as a preliminary cross-discipline check rather than a definitive economics distribution.

The companion "Star Wars" dataset (Brodeur, Lé, Sangnier & Zylberberg 2016; 50,000 tests from AER, JPE, QJE, 2005--2011) [@brodeur2016] is available in our archive but its public CSV does not include a sample-size column, so those 17,883 test-level observations cannot currently be placed on the log-*N* vs log-*d* axes of @fig-published-d-vs-n. They would contribute a classic Brodeur *z*-curve (density of test statistics) but not a scatter. Recovering *N* from the original ICPSR Stata file (openicpsr project 113633) is pending; it would expand the economics overlay by roughly two orders of magnitude.

### Havránek EIS micro subset (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#havranek-eis-micro-subset-figure-2-gold-squares}

41 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Havránek [@havranek2015] meta-analysis of the elasticity of intertemporal substitution, which harvested 2,735 EIS estimates from 169 studies published in economics journals through 2014. The raw meta-file mixes two incompatible study designs: macro time-series work where *N* counts quarters of aggregate consumption, and micro panel work where *N* counts individual households. Only the micro subset is comparable to psychology's statcheck corpus and to the Brodeur causal-inference corpus, where *N* counts experimental units.

The filter applied to build the @fig-published-d-vs-n overlay proceeded in three steps. First, restrict to `micro == 1` --- 41 studies using household-level panel data where *N* = individuals --- leaving 512 estimates. Second, winsorize to \|EIS\| ≤ 5; this drops 24 of 512 estimates (4.7%), all implausible-magnitude outliers for an elasticity parameter whose true value theory and micro evidence put in roughly \[0, 1\]. Third, convert each estimate to a Cohen's-*d* analogue using *d* = 2\|*t*\|/√*N* with *t* = EIS/SE, the same conversion used for *t* rows in the statcheck corpus and for test statistics in the Brodeur corpus. Estimates were then collapsed to study-level medians (median \|*d*\| at median *N* within each study), paralleling the paper-level collapse used for the blue and the other-green point clouds. This yields 41 paper-level points; three fall below the plot's *d* = 0.01 floor and are omitted from the visible scatter.

Sub-corpus profile in isolation: median *N* = 5,704 individuals, median \|*d*\| = 0.072, 66% above *p* \< 0.05, standalone log--log slope −0.55 (*r* = −0.79). The cleaned micro subset with columns `estimate_id, study_id, study, country, eis, se, sample_size, abs_t, d_proxy, is_micro, is_panel, ols, tsls, ml, bayes, pubyear` is available as `havranek_eis_comparable.csv` in the project's downloadable artifacts (486 rows, 41 studies).

### Gechert--Havránek σ meta-analysis (@fig-published-d-vs-n, green circles) {#gechert-havranek-sigma-figure-2-gold-squares}

121 of the green circles in @fig-published-d-vs-n come from the Gechert--Havránek--Iršová--Kolcunová [@gechert2022] meta-analysis of the capital-labor elasticity of substitution σ, published in the *Review of Economics and Statistics* (2022). The meta-file contains 3,186 estimates from 121 studies spanning 1961--2017, harvested from production-function and factor-demand regressions in the macroeconomics and growth literatures. Unlike the EIS dataset, the σ dataset does not separate micro from macro study designs along a single indicator; it instead carries three flags (`is_panel`, `is_crosssec`, `is_timeseries`) identifying the data structure behind each estimate. For the @fig-published-d-vs-n overlay we did not filter by design; the 121 paper-level medians include all three design types.

The filter applied was: winsorize to \|σ\| ≤ 5 (drops \~0.8% of estimates as implausible magnitudes, since σ is bounded below by 0 and above by \~2 for plausible production technologies); convert to Cohen's-*d* analogue via *d* = 2\|*t*\|/√*N* with *t* = σ/SE; collapse to study-level medians. Sub-corpus profile in isolation: median *N* = 44, median \|*d*\|  = 1.48, 87% above *p* \< 0.05, standalone log--log slope  −0.41 (*r* = −0.55). Note the very small median *N*: σ studies often estimate production functions on country--year or industry--year panels with dozens rather than thousands of observations, which places them in the same sample-size regime as psychology and at the small-*N* extreme of the econ overlay. The cleaned subset with columns `study_id, study, country, sigma, se, sample_size, abs_t, d_proxy, is_panel, is_crosssec, is_timeseries, pubyear` is available as `havranek_sigma_comparable.csv` in the project's downloadable artifacts (3,161 rows, 121 studies).

**Robustness note across the three econ sub-corpora.** The three standalone slopes are −0.45 (Brodeur), −0.41 (σ), and −0.55 (EIS). The combined slope across all 272 paper-level points is -0.55 (*r* = -0.84). The EIS slope is steeper than the pure-filter prediction of −0.5; the other two match it within noise. The EIS case is consistent with residual heterogeneity in true EIS values correlated with study design (smaller EIS studies can load onto sub-literatures with larger true effects or tighter instruments), which is additive to the filter slope rather than substitutive. Cluster-robust standard errors on `study_id` are warranted if anyone wants a confidence interval on the combined econ slope; one estimate = one observation here overweights studies that contribute many estimates, but that does not bias the slope estimate itself, only its standard error.

*The upshot of Appendix D: the ratio of original-to-replication effect sizes is not a bug at the edges of psychology. It is the central tendency of the literature.*

## Appendix E --- Material retired from the main flow (pending placement) {#appendix-e-placeholder}

The four subsections below were previously cited by figures in the main text. The figures they document have been removed from the current flow; the data and notes are kept here so the analysis is not lost. Disposition of this material is still being decided --- likely some of it will return as standalone figures (the Cochrane medicine data, for example, needs a provenance-stratified treatment before it can be used; see the companion essay on the Cochrane p-hacking debate) and some of it may stay as reference material only.

### Preregistered hypotheses: Scheel 2021 + PSA-CR001 corpus

These rows are the single-study preregistered hypotheses from the Scheel / Lakens and PSA-CR001 corpora. They were previously displayed as a purple dashed curve alongside the 555 originals and replications; in the current flow they are not plotted. They are not original--replication pairs, so they do not appear in the main replication-pair table above. Rows 1--32 are from Scheel, Schijen & Lakens [@scheel2021]: each is one hypothesis from a published psychology Registered Report, showing what the preregistered confirmatory test returned with no replication attempt. Rows 33--36 are from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022) [@dorison2022]: each is one of four primary preregistered hypotheses tested across 52 countries, pooled to a single effect size and sample count. Supported = the hypothesis was judged supported by the authors of the original report.

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 40. Preregistered hypotheses: Scheel 2021 + PSA-CR001 corpus**

| # | Study | N | \|d\| | Supported | Journal |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 1 | Kuppuraj 2018 — Online statistical learning reliability | 42 | 1.80 | yes | Royal Society Open Science |
| 2 | Grubb 2018 — Former targets capture | 80 | 1.10 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 3 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp3 | 20 | 1.09 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 4 | Voorspoels 2014 — Coalition encoding + cue | 463 | 0.85 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 5 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Liars liked less | 155 | 0.60 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 6 | Dutilh 2017 — WM drift rate hard | 916 | 0.58 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 7 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Liars trusted less | 155 | 0.56 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 8 | Dutilh 2017 — WM drift rate easy | 916 | 0.49 | yes | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 9 | Rotteveel 2015 — Lever congruency Exp2 | 50 | 0.49 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 10 | Steegen 2014 — Guess averaging immediate | 471 | 0.40 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 11 | Rowland 2014 — RIF Exp2 | 140 | 0.38 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 12 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food MTurk | 366 | 0.38 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 13 | Verkoeijen 2014 — Spacing inductive learning | 123 | 0.37 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 14 | Evers 2014 — Diagnosticity Study 1b | 199 | 0.36 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 15 | Rotteveel 2015 — Lever congruency Exp1 | 99 | 0.35 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 16 | Steegen 2014 — Guess averaging delayed | 140 | 0.34 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 17 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food SG | 198 | 0.32 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 18 | Rowland 2014 — RIF Exp1 | 72 | 0.31 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 19 | van ’t Veer 2015 — Skin temp observing liar | 155 | 0.31 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 20 | Kvetnaya 2018 — Irrelevant speech spatial | 40 | 0.29 | no | J European Psych Students |
| 21 | Bombari 2017 — Body posture felt power | 200 | 0.28 | yes | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 22 | Grubb 2018 — Feedback capture diff | 80 | 0.20 | no | Attention Perception Psychophysics |
| 23 | Voorspoels 2014 — Race encoding + coalition cue | 463 | 0.18 | yes | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 24 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp2 | 24 | 0.17 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 25 | Jackson 2017 — Posture self-concept size | 128 | 0.15 | no | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 26 | Bombari 2017 — Body posture gambling | 200 | 0.08 | no | Comprehensive Results in Social Psychology |
| 27 | Zanolie 2014 — Magnitude×side Exp1 | 20 | 0.08 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 28 | Evers 2014 — Diagnosticity Study 1a | 253 | 0.06 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 29 | Rowland 2014 — Cue-independent RIF Exp3 | 67 | 0.05 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
| 30 | Johnson 2014 — Cleanliness moral judgment Exp1 | 208 | 0.01 | no | Social Psychology |
| 31 | Johnson 2014 — Cleanliness moral judgment Exp2 | 126 | 0.01 | no | Social Psychology |
| 32 | Ong 2015 — Comfort food NL | 176 | 0.01 | no | Frontiers in Psychology |
:::

### Added preregistered rows from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022)

::: {.paper-table}
**Table 41. Added preregistered rows from PSA-CR001 (Dorison et al. 2022)**

| # | Primary hypothesis (preregistered) | Pooled N | Pooled \|d\| | Supported |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| 33 | Behavioral intentions (loss vs gain framing) | 15,772 | 0.10 | no |
| 34 | Policy support: individual autonomy subscale | 15,772 | 0.07 | no |
| 35 | Policy support: government power subscale | 15,772 | 0.08 | no |
| 36 | Information seeking (loss vs gain framing) | 15,772 | 0.02 | no |
